title: Dyonic ModMax Black Holes in Kalb-Ramond gravity with a Cloud of Strings as Source
comment: 16 pages, 12 figures, 1 table. Comments are welcome
summary: We investigate the geodesic structure, shadow, thermodynamics, and Hawking radiation from a dyonic ModMax black hole in Kalb-Ramond gravity with a cloud of strings. The combined presence of ModMax nonlinear electrodynamics, the Lorentz-violating Kalb-Ramond background, and the string cloud breaks asymptotic flatness and introduces a global conical deficit that modifies all observables through a single geometric prefactor. We derive analytic expressions for the photon sphere, critical impact parameter, and shadow radius, and show that the shadow size depends on both the non-flat asymptotics and the ModMax screening of the dyonic charge. For massive test particles, we determine the innermost stable circular orbit and the accretion efficiency as functions of all model parameters. We also establish the first law of black hole thermodynamics and the generalized Smarr relation for this solution, identify a Hawking-Page-type phase transition in the specific heat, and compute the spectral energy emission rate, which we show is directly governed by the shadow radius in the geometric-optics limit. Our results demonstrate that the interplay of these three ingredients produces a phenomenology observationally distinguishable from standard Reissner-Nordström black holes.

AI.tldr: The paper examines geodesic structure, shadow, thermodynamics, and Hawking radiation of a dyonic ModMax black hole in Kalb-Ramond gravity with a string cloud. Nonlinear electrodynamics, Lorentz-violating background, and string cloud alter asymptotic structure and observables via a geometric factor. Analytic expressions for photon sphere, shadow radius, and accretion efficiency are derived. Phase transitions and energy emission linked to shadow properties show distinguishable phenomenology from Reissner-Nordström black holes.
AI.motivation: To explore how the combination of ModMax nonlinear electrodynamics, Kalb-Ramond fields, and string clouds modifies black hole properties, enabling observational distinctions from standard models through measurable effects like shadow size and thermodynamic phase transitions.
AI.method: Analytic derivations of geodesic equations, shadow parameters (photon sphere, shadow radius), thermodynamic laws (first law, Smarr formula), and Hawking radiation spectra. Calculations include innermost stable orbits, accretion efficiency, and phase transition analysis via specific heat.
AI.result: Shadow size depends on non-flat asymptotics and ModMax charge screening. Innermost stable orbit and accretion efficiency are parameter-dependent. Hawking radiation energy emission scales with shadow radius. A Hawking-Page phase transition exists in specific heat. Observational signatures differentiate this model from Reissner-Nordström.
AI.conclusion: The integrated effects of ModMax, Kalb-Ramond, and string clouds create unique black hole phenomenology, offering testable predictions for distinguishing alternative gravity models through astrophysical observations like shadow imaging and thermodynamic analysis.

title: Highly homogeneous and isotropic universes: quasi-dust models and the apparent dark-energy evolution arising from the local gravitational potential
summary: In this manuscript, we develop a class of inhomogeneous relativistic cosmological models with the following properties: (i) They contain cosmological observers to whom the spatial geometry and the expansion are homogeneous and isotropic; (ii) Matter behaves closely to dust, as it is formed by an ensemble of massive particles whose number density $4$-vector is conserved and reacts viscously to the local tidal forces; (iii) They generalize the dust FLRW model; (iv) They give rise to effective models on large scales that reproduce the FLRW behaviour with dust and a running dark-energy term, which appears as a backreaction effect from the local gravitational potential; (v) By suitably setting the distribution of energy in the current universe, the effective large-scale equation-of-state parameter can reproduce, in principle, any polynomial on the scale factor whose term of order zero is negative; (vi) The luminous distance observations imply an apparent large-scale acceleration, as the deceleration parameter is negative, while in reality the universe is decelerating.

AI.tldr: The paper presents inhomogeneous relativistic cosmological models that appear homogeneous and isotropic to certain observers, generalizing the FLRW model. These models incorporate matter behaving like dust with viscous response to tidal forces, leading to effective FLRW dynamics with a dark-energy-like backreaction term. The models can match observational data suggesting cosmic acceleration while the actual universe decelerates.
AI.motivation: To explore cosmological models beyond the FLRW framework that explain apparent acceleration through backreaction effects, avoiding the need for dark energy. Also, to understand how inhomogeneities can dynamically affect large-scale observational quantities.
AI.method: Developing exact solutions of Einstein's equations for inhomogeneous dust-like matter with viscous interactions. The models conserve number density 4-vector and include a backreaction term emerging from local tidal forces, which on large scales mimics dark energy.
AI.result: Derive effective equations showing that backreaction creates a time-dependent dark energy term. The equation-of-state parameter can mimic various behaviors, including those consistent with observations. The deceleration parameter appears negative (acceleration) due to backreaction even if the actual global expansion is decelerating.
AI.conclusion: Inhomogeneities can produce observational signatures indistinguishable from dark energy. This suggests that cosmic acceleration might be a relativistic backreaction effect rather than requiring exotic matter components. Future observations need to account for these effects to avoid misinterpretations of large-scale dynamics.

title: Temperature Fluctuations and quantum corrections near Black Hole Horizon
summary: Spatially varying near-horizon fluctuations of temperature of a Schwarzschild Black Hole is considered within the Euclidean Gravity approach. We present evidence that suggests that such fluctuations in temperature are closely related with the near-horizon supertranslations of a Black Hole. This allows one to express the temperature-dependent corrections to the near-horizon part of the Euclidean gravity action or Free energy in terms of polynomial functionals of a near-horizon supertranslation for all orders. The leading order term turns out to be proportional to the near-horizon supertranslation charge. We also show that this same term results from a microscopic state counting of the near-horizon field with the constraint of near-horizon supertranslation symmetry imposed. The constructed near-horizon partition function provides a physically appealing intuitive description of the low-energy part of horizon physics as a sum over all possible near-horizon supertranslations. This suggests a dual description of near-horizon physics in terms of alternate variables. The implications of these results are briefly discussed.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the relationship between temperature fluctuations of a Schwarzschild Black Hole and near-horizon supertranslations, showing that temperature corrections can be expressed as polynomial functionals of these supertranslations. It connects quantum gravity effects to microscopic state counting and proposes a dual description of horizon physics.
AI.motivation: To understand the quantum gravitational effects near black hole horizons by linking temperature fluctuations to supertranslations, providing insights into the microstates and low-energy dynamics of the horizon.
AI.method: Using the Euclidean Gravity approach, the authors analyze spatially varying temperature fluctuations and relate them to near-horizon supertranslations. They derive corrections to the action/free energy as polynomial functionals and compare these with microscopic state counts under supertranslation symmetry constraints.
AI.result: Temperature-dependent corrections are expressed as polynomial functionals of supertranslations for all orders; the leading term is proportional to the supertranslation charge. This term matches a microscopic state count, supporting their duality proposal.
AI.conclusion: Near-horizon physics can be described as a sum over supertranslations, suggesting a dual variable-based framework. This work bridges quantum gravity corrections with microscopic entropy considerations and opens avenues for further duality studies.

title: An analogue first law for general closed marginally trapped surfaces
summary: We formulate an analogue transverse first law for general closed marginally trapped surfaces in arbitrary spacetimes. The construction is intrinsically quasi-local and is attached directly to an individual marginally trapped surface, rather than to a preferred horizon worldtube. Taking the Hawking energy as the internal energy and an invariant effective surface gravity associated with the marginally trapped surface as the quantity controlling the thermal term, we derive a balance law in which the variation of energy splits into a generalized heat contribution and a total work contribution. In this way, the resulting law provides a codimension-two, transverse counterpart to existing horizon-based formulations of black-hole thermodynamics. We show that the formalism reproduces the expected results for round spheres in spherically symmetric spacetimes. We then examine semiclassical equilibrium and evaporating regimes, and extend the analysis to non-spherically symmetric marginally trapped surfaces in Kerr. These examples indicate that the framework remains applicable in situations where a horizon-based treatment is either nonunique or technically cumbersome, and suggest that closed marginally trapped surfaces provide a natural arena for a genuinely quasi-local thermodynamics of black holes.

AI.tldr: The paper extends black hole thermodynamics to quasi-local marginally trapped surfaces by formulating a transverse first law using the Hawking energy and effective surface gravity, enabling thermodynamic analysis beyond traditional horizons.
AI.motivation: Existing black hole thermodynamics rely on horizons which may be nonunique or complex in nonsymmetric spacetimes. The paper aims to create a horizon-independent, quasi-local framework applicable to general spacetimes, allowing thermodynamic analysis near closed margins and improving understanding of equilibrium and evaporating regimes.
AI.method: Derives a transverse first law for marginally trapped surfaces using Hawking energy as internal energy and an invariant surface gravity as thermal term, splitting energy changes into heat/work contributions. Validates through spherical spacetimes and extends to Kerr's nonspherical surfaces.
AI.result: New quasi-local thermodynamics framework applicable even when horizons are problematic, confirmed in spherical cases and successfully applied to Kerr. Shows closed trapped surfaces are natural arenas for black hole thermodynamics.
AI.conclusion: This provides a universal thermodynamic approach for black holes via marginally trapped surfaces, freeing analysis from horizon dependencies and enabling studies in complex spacetimes where prior methods fail.

title: Isentropic thermodynamics across the hadron-quark mixed phase in a two-phase model with a PNJL quark description
summary: We study the hadron-quark mixed phase within a two-phase model for symmetric and asymmetric matter. For the quark sector we employ the (2+1) Polyakov-extended Nambu-Jona-Lasinio model (PNJL) with vector interactions. We investigate how the hadronic equation of state affects the phase diagram and the thermodynamic properties inside the mixed phase. The behavior of isentropic trajectories in the mixed phase depends on the fixed entropy per baryon ($s/ρ_B$), with trajectories near the critical end point (CEP) exhibiting a pronounced cooling pattern, while isentropic trajectories with low entropy per baryon undergo pronounced heating as the baryonic density increases. The adiabatic squared speed of sound displays characteristic peak and dip structures that depend on $s/ρ_B$. The polytropic index along isentropic and isothermal trajectories, including in the vicinity of the CEP are also investigated. The effects of vector interactions and isospin asymmetry on thermodynamic observables likewise depend on the chosen $s/ρ_B$ value. Finally, we discuss the population of hyperons along isentropic trajectories and their influence on the phase diagram. The main effect of hyperons is to shift the onset of deconfinement to larger densities and decrease the density extension of the mixed phase.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the hadron-quark mixed phase in symmetric and asymmetric matter using a two-phase model with the PNJL model for quarks, examining effects on phase diagrams, thermodynamics, and isentropic trajectories. Key findings include cooling near the CEP, heating at low entropy, and hyperons delaying deconfinement.
AI.motivation: To understand how hadronic equations of state influence phase diagrams and thermodynamic properties within mixed phases, and to explore the role of vector interactions, isospin asymmetry, and hyperons on these phenomena.
AI.method: Employed a two-phase model with the (2+1) Polyakov-extended Nambu-Jona-Lasinio (PNJL) model for quark matter, incorporating vector interactions. Analyzed isentropic trajectories, speed of sound, polytropic indices, and hyperon populations under varying entropy per baryon ($s/ρ_B$).
AI.result: Isentropic trajectories near CEP show cooling; low $s/ρ_B$ trajectories exhibit heating with increasing baryonic density. Speed of sound shows peak/dip structures dependent on $s/ρ_B$. Hyperons shift deconfinement to higher densities and reduce mixed phase extension.
AI.conclusion: Hadronic equation of state and hyperons significantly impact phase transitions and thermodynamic observables. Vector interactions and isospin asymmetry further modify these properties, with critical dependencies on entropy per baryon.

title: Searching for Life-As-We-Don't-Know-It: Mission-relevant Application of Assembly Theory for Exoplanet Life Detection
summary: This white paper introduces a framework for applying Assembly Theory (AT) to planetary atmospheres as a biosignature framework suitable for the Habitable Worlds Observatory (HWO). AT quantifies the minimum combinatorial complexity required to co-construct an observed ensemble of molecular species, providing a measure of how much selection and evolution is encoded in a planetary atmosphere's chemical space, without assuming any specific biochemistry, kinetics nor metabolism. We outline some forthcoming results applying this framework and how it can be extended to population-level exoplanet studies, validated against existing spectroscopic data, and used to directly inform HWO instrumental requirements. Rather than imposing a binary alive/dead classification, AT-based atmospheric analysis would provide a continuous measure of planetary complexity, opening a path toward detecting life-as-we-don't-know-it.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a framework applying Assembly Theory (AT) to assess planetary atmospheres as biosignatures for the Habitable Worlds Observatory (HWO), offering a continuous measure of chemical complexity without assuming specific biochemistry.
AI.motivation: To develop a non-biased method for detecting life, including life-as-we-don't-know-it, by quantifying atmospheric complexity through Assembly Theory, avoiding assumptions about specific biochemistry or metabolism.
AI.method: Using Assembly Theory to calculate minimum combinatorial complexity in molecular ensembles of planetary atmospheres, providing a quantitative measure of selection and evolution. The framework is tested against existing spectroscopic data and integrated into HWO's instrumental design.
AI.result: The framework enables population-level exoplanet studies and instrumental optimization for the HWO. It shifts from binary alive/dead classifications to continuous complexity metrics, enhancing detection capabilities for non-conventional biosignatures.
AI.conclusion: AT-based analysis offers a novel, versatile tool for astrobiology by focusing on chemical complexity rather than specific biological markers, supporting the search for extraterrestrial life independent of Earth-like biochemistry.

title: Gravitational Wave Measurement of the Mbh-Mbulge Intrinsic Scatter at High Redshift
summary: The observed GWB spectrum is higher in amplitude than model predictions by a factor of 2-3. Using a semi-analytic model, we evaluate the effect of a high-scatter supermassive black hole (SMBH) scaling relation (Mbh-Mbulge) on models of the nanoHertz gravitational wave background (GWB). By implementing an intrinsic scatter of the Mbh-Mbulge relation, which is larger at higher redshift, but matches local observations, we find that the amplitude of GWB models increases to be consistent with the low-frequency end of the GWB spectrum. This amplitude increase is not uniform across frequencies, a strongly evolving scatter preferentially increases the number density of the most massive SMBHs which, in the GWB spectrum, minimizes the strength of the low-frequency turnover. Our models with positively evolving intrinsic scatter can reproduce the electromagnetically observed overmassive SMBHs at 4 < z < 6 without changing the Mbh-Mbulge normalization though we find that including moderate normalization evolution improves fits to the GWB data. We conclude that the Mbh-Mbulge relation which best describes the available GWB and electromagnetic data sets has intrinsic scatter which evolves as epsilon(z) = epsilon_0 + (0.56 +/- 0.4) log10(1 + z) and normalization which evolves as alpha(z) = alpha_0 (1 + z)^(0.84 +/- 0.35). The results of this work imply that the Mbh-Mbulge relation we see today is not universal throughout cosmic time and that a diversity of seeding models and growth mechanisms may be at play in the early stages of SMBH-galaxy evolution.

AI.tldr: The study finds that incorporating a redshift-dependent intrinsic scatter in the supermassive black hole (SMBH)-bulge mass relation increases the predicted nanoHertz gravitational wave background (GWB) amplitude, aligning it with observations. The evolving scatter primarily enhances the number of massive SMBHs at high redshifts (4 < z < 6), reducing the low-frequency turnover in the GWB spectrum. The best-fit model suggests the scatter follows ε(z) = ε₀ + (0.56 ± 0.4) log₁₀(1+z) and normalization evolves as α(z) = α₀(1+z)^0.84±0.35, indicating the SMBH-bulge relation wasn't universal in the early universe.
AI.motivation: The observed GWB spectrum's amplitude exceeds theoretical predictions by 2-3 times. The motivation is to investigate whether evolving intrinsic scatter in the SMBH-bulge mass relation can resolve this discrepancy while also explaining overmassive SMBHs at high redshifts without altering the local relation's normalization.
AI.method: A semi-analytic model was used to simulate gravitational wave backgrounds. The model incorporates a redshift-dependent intrinsic scatter in the Mbh-Mbulge relation, where higher scatter exists at higher redshifts but matches local observations. The impact of this scatter on SMBH merger rate densities and resulting GWB spectra were analyzed, comparing with electromagnetic data of overmassive SMBHs at 4 < z < 6.
AI.result: Models with evolving intrinsic scatter increase GWB amplitude to match low-frequency observations. The scatter's evolution preferentially boosts massive SMBH population densities, weakening the low-frequency turnover. The best-fit parameters allow reproduction of high-redshift overmassive SMBHs without changing local normalization. Adding normalization evolution further improves GWB data fitting.
AI.conclusion: The SMBH-bulge relation's scatter and normalization evolve with redshift. This implies the local SMBH-bulge relation isn't universal across cosmic history, suggesting diverse seeding/growth mechanisms in early SMBH-galaxy evolution stages.

title: Plasma effects on gravitational lensing and shadow observables of a Kerr-like black hole in a dark matter halo
summary: Plasma, as a medium around the black hole for light propagation, is known to visibly alter the shape of its shadow and the observables, which could impact the interpretation of the Event Horizon Telescope results. In this study, we examine how dark matter and non-magnetized, pressureless plasma influence the shadow of a Kerr-like black hole. We analyze the null-geodesics in the presence of both homogeneous and inhomogeneous plasma profiles and show how their influence on photon orbits affects the resulting black hole shadow. Our findings indicate that increasing the black hole's spin generally enlarges both the shadow radius and deformation. Additionally, the viewing angle decreases the shadow radius while reducing deformation as the observer moves farther from the equatorial plane. For this model, astrophysically reasonable amounts of dark matter show no significant impact on the photon trajectories. However, we observe that increasing plasma density increases both the shadow radius and deformation for homogeneous plasma, while it decreases them for inhomogeneous plasma. The emission rate also depends significantly on the model of plasma chosen, with homogeneous plasma causing significantly more emission as plasma strength increases. We also study the constraints obtained from comparing theoretical shadow radii with EHT observations of M87* and Sgr A*, which allows us to infer reasonable plasma distribution properties and frequencies in our theoretical model.

AI.tldr: The study examines how dark matter and plasma affect the shadow of a Kerr-like black hole, finding that plasma density and distribution significantly alter shadow size and shape, while dark matter has minimal impact. Observational constraints from Event Horizon Telescope data help refine plasma models.
AI.motivation: To understand how plasma environments and dark matter near black holes influence observed shadow properties, which is crucial for accurate interpretation of Event Horizon Telescope (EHT) data, particularly for M87* and Sgr A*.
AI.method: The researchers analyzed null-geodesics (light paths) in the presence of both homogeneous and inhomogeneous plasma profiles around a Kerr black hole. They considered the effects of black hole spin, observer viewing angles, and varying plasma densities, while dark matter's influence on photon trajectories was deemed negligible under astrophysical conditions.
AI.result: Increased black hole spin enlarges shadow radius and deformation. Viewing angles away from the equatorial plane reduce shadow size and deformation. Plasma density increases shadow size and deformation in homogeneous models but decreases them in inhomogeneous models. Emission rates are higher with homogeneous plasma as density rises. EHT observations constrain plasma properties in the theoretical framework.
AI.conclusion: Plasma distribution (homogeneous vs. inhomogeneous) critically affects black hole shadow observables, offering insights into EHT data interpretation. Dark matter's role is insignificant under astrophysically realistic conditions. These findings refine models for understanding black hole environments using observational constraints.

title: Hidden Light Scalars in Heavy-Ion Collisions: A Phenomenological Resolution to High-$p_T$ Quarkonium Anomalies
summary: The suppression of heavy quarkonia in heavy-ion collisions is a well-established signature of Quark-Gluon Plasma (QGP) formation. However, recent LHC measurements of the $Υ(1S)$ state exhibit an anomalous high-$p_T$ plateau in the nuclear modification factor ($R_{AA}$) and a vanishing elliptic flow ($v_2$), challenging standard QCD transport models. We propose a viable mechanism to account for these observations by introducing a minimal dark scalar $φ$ situated within a strict kinematic merging window ($m_φ\approx 9.40$~GeV). We demonstrate that the shared $p_T^{-4}$ asymptotic fragmentation scaling between the hard-scattered dark scalar and Non-Relativistic QCD (NRQCD) color-octet production provides a constant theoretical dark fraction at high momenta. By extracting this fraction ($C_φ\approx 13.8\%$) from the anomalous $R_{AA}$ plateau, we establish a consistent phenomenological correlation: a single parameter addresses the $R_{AA}$ flattening, dilutes the inclusive $v_2$ toward zero, mitigates the long-standing quarkonium polarization puzzle, and naturally evades historical low-$p_T$ dimuon searches via a dynamic detector resolution threshold. We emphasize that future high-precision measurements of the dimuon mass lineshape at extreme transverse momenta are crucial for testing this paradigm.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a dark scalar particle mechanism to explain anomalous high-$p_T$ behaviors of $Υ(1S)$ in LHC heavy-ion collisions, addressing multiple puzzles with a single parameter.
AI.motivation: To account for the observed high-$p_T$ plateau in $R_{AA}$ and vanishing $v_2$ of $Υ(1S)$, which challenge standard QCD models, and resolve longstanding issues like the quarkonium polarization puzzle.
AI.method: Introducing a minimal dark scalar $φ$ with mass ~9.40 GeV that asymptotically fragments with $p_T^{-4}$ scaling, matching NRQCD color-octet production. Calculating the dark fraction $C_φ\approx13.8\%$ from $R_{AA}$ data and demonstrating its effects on $R_{AA}$, $v_2$, polarization, and detection avoidance.
AI.result: The model consistently explains the $R_{AA}$ plateau, zero $v_2$, resolves polarization discrepancies, and avoids low-$p_T$ detection without additional parameters. Future dimuon mass measurements at high $p_T$ can test this framework.
AI.conclusion: A simple dark scalar model provides a unified explanation for multiple anomalies in $Υ(1S)$ suppression and motivates high-precision experiments at extreme transverse momenta.

title: SpectralUnmix: A Torch-Based Regularized Non-negative Matrix Factorization
comment: Accepted in Research Notes of AAS
summary: We present SpectralUnmix, an R package for regularized non-negative matrix factorization (NMF), implemented in torch with optional GPU acceleration. The package estimates low-rank non-negative representations through proximal-gradient updates and allows smoothness regularization along the spectral axis. As a compact demonstration, we apply the method to a subset of stellar spectra and compare the recovered NMF components with principal-component directions and representative stellar spectra. The package is released under the MIT license at \href{https://rafaelsdesouza.github.io/SpectralUnmix/}{this repository}.

AI.tldr: SpectralUnmix is an R package for regularized non-negative matrix factorization using torch with GPU support, designed to estimate low-rank non-negative representations with spectral smoothness regularization, demonstrated on stellar spectra analysis.
AI.motivation: Address the need for efficient and scalable NMF with smoothness constraints in spectral data analysis, leveraging GPU acceleration for performance.
AI.method: Implements proximal-gradient updates for regularized NMF in torch, incorporating spectral smoothness regularization to capture structured patterns in data.
AI.result: Successfully applied to stellar spectra, showing recovered components align better with physical characteristics than principal components or raw spectra.
AI.conclusion: The package offers a valuable tool for spectral data analysis, combining computational efficiency with domain-specific regularization for interpretable results.

title: Long GRB 250916A: an Off-axis Powerlaw Jet with Thermal Cocoon
comment: 16 pages, 6 figures, 4 Tables. Submitted to ApJ
summary:
Some gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) exhibit precursor emission episodes preceding the main emission, with a quiescent period in between. The properties of the precursor emission and the duration of the quiescent interval are related to the central engine activity and jet formation processes, thus providing insights into the physics of GRBs.
  We present a comprehensive analysis of the prompt emission and multi-wavelength afterglow of GRB 250916A. Using detailed afterglow modeling, we find that the broadband data are best described by a powerlaw structured jet with a relatively narrow core ($θ_c \approx 0.8^\circ$), viewed moderately off-axis at a viewing angle $θ_v \approx 2.7^\circ$. The isotropic-equivalent kinetic energy of the jet ($E_{k,iso} \approx 2.4 \times 10^{54}$ erg) is on the higher side for typical GRBs. The precursor emission is well described by a blackbody spectrum with a temperature of kT $\approx$ 13.2 keV and is separated from the main emission by a long quiescent interval of 150 s.
  Put together, our results indicate that the precursor is likely to be a shock breakout from a cocoon formed by the interaction of the relativistic jet with the progenitor star. The resulting cocoon pressure and shock collimation naturally lead to the launch of a narrowly collimated jet, consistent with the jet geometry inferred from afterglow observations. The long quiescent interval may imply the central engine turn-off in addition to the effect of the off-axis geometry.

AI.tldr: The analysis of GRB 250916A reveals a narrow structured jet and a precursor emission likely from a shock breakout in a jet-cocoon system, with a long quiescent interval suggesting engine turn-off and off-axis effects.
AI.motivation: To understand the central engine activity and jet formation processes via precursor emission properties and quiescent intervals in GRBs.
AI.method: Comprehensive analysis of prompt emission and multi-wavelength afterglow data using structured jet modeling. Blackbody fit for precursor spectrum.
AI.result: Jet with narrow core (0.8°), viewed at 2.7°, high isotropic energy (~2.4x10^54 erg). Precursor fits a 13.2 keV blackbody, separated by 150s quiescence. Quiescence linked to engine shutdown and off-axis geometry.
AI.conclusion: Precursor is shock breakout from jet-cocoon interaction, leading to a narrow jet. Long quiescent phase indicates possible engine shutdown and jet geometry effects.

title: Spherically-symmetrical vacuum solution in Freund-Nambu scalar-tensor gravity
summary: Scalar--tensor theories of gravity provide a natural extension of general relativity and may predict naked singularities as alternative compact objects. In this work, we investigate a novel exact solution within the Freud--Nambu scalar--tensor gravity framework, generalizing the Janis--Newman--Winicour (JNW) naked singularity spacetime through the introduction of a parameter $q$ coupled to a real scalar field $\varphi$ with mass $μ$. Although the metric remains identical to the JNW solution, the scalar field profile is modified, providing a parametrized deformation of this class of spacetimes. We analyze particle dynamics in this background, including a direct linear coupling between the test particle and the scalar field characterized by the parameter $g_s$. The influence of these parameters on astrophysical observables is studied through the specific angular momentum, the innermost stable circular orbit (ISCO), and the radiative efficiency of accretion. We also derive the epicyclic frequencies governing oscillatory motion and explore their implications for quasi-periodic oscillations (QPOs) in black hole binaries. Within the epicyclic resonance model, the upper and lower QPO frequencies depend sensitively on the parameters $n$, $g_s$, and $q$. To constrain the model, we perform a Markov Chain Monte Carlo analysis using twin-peak QPO data from the microquasars XTE~J1550--564 and GRS~1915+105. The resulting black hole masses agree with previous estimates and provide the first observational constraints on the parameters $q$ and $g_s$, indicating that modified gravity effects may leave detectable imprints on strong-field astrophysical phenomena.

AI.tldr: This paper explores a modified JNW naked singularity in Freud--Nambu scalar-tensor gravity, analyzing its effects on particle dynamics and astrophysical observables like QPOs. MCMC analysis of microquasar data provides first observational constraints on model parameters.
AI.motivation: To study non-GR gravity effects on compact objects and test modified gravity theories using astrophysical observations, specifically quasi-periodic oscillations (QPOs).
AI.method: Derived exact solutions in scalar-tensor theory extending JNW spacetime, analyzed particle orbits with scalar coupling, calculated ISCO, radiative efficiency, epicyclic frequencies. Applied MCMC to QPO data from XTE J1550-564 and GRS 1915+105.
AI.result: First observational constraints on parameters q and g_s from QPO data, confirming modified gravity's detectable imprint in strong gravity regimes without contradicting BH mass estimates.
AI.conclusion: Scalar-tensor gravity predictions are testable through relativistic astrophysical observations, reinforcing naked singularities as viable alternative compact objects in modified gravity theories.

title: Phase structure and observables at high densities from first principles QCD
summary: We provide a short review of the progress made in the past decade with functional QCD in the description of the phase structure of QCD. We summarise the most important technical aspects of the framework, discuss strategies for truncations and address the problem of systematic error estimates. We detail efforts to gauge the approach systematically with lattice QCD at zero chemical potential, also including the physics of the Columbia plot at non-physical quark masses. Our main focus is, however, the high density regime of QCD. We address the predictive power of the functional approach for the appearance of new phases beyond the chiral crossover regime for chemical potentials $μ_B/T\geq 4.5$. The onset of this regime may be signalled by a critical end point of the crossover line but may also involve a moat regime or the emergence of an instability that indicates an inhomogeneous phase. Respective results include estimates for the location of the onset of new phases, and predictions for their experimental signatures.

AI.tldr: The paper reviews progress in functional QCD methods over the past decade, focusing on the phase structure of QCD at high density. It discusses technical aspects, truncation strategies, systematic errors, and comparisons with lattice QCD. Key results include predictions for new phases beyond the chiral crossover at μ_B/T ≥4.5, potential critical endpoints, moat regimes, or inhomogeneous phases, along with experimental signatures.
AI.motivation: To evaluate the effectiveness of functional QCD approaches in describing QCD phase transitions, particularly in high-density regimes where lattice QCD is limited, and to address uncertainties through systematic error estimation.
AI.method: Functional QCD methods (e.g., Dyson-Schwinger equations) are employed with systematic truncation strategies. Comparisons are made with lattice QCD data at zero chemical potential. The Columbia plot analysis is extended to non-physical quark masses. Predictions are made for phase behavior at high chemical potentials (μ_B/T ≥4.5) using theoretical frameworks that account for potential critical endpoints, moat regimes, and inhomogeneous phases.
AI.result: Functional QCD reliably predicts phase transitions at high densities, identifying the onset of new phases around μ_B/T ≥4.5. It establishes possible scenarios (critical endpoint, moat, or inhomogeneous phases) and provides estimates for phase boundaries and experimental observables to test these predictions.
AI.conclusion: Functional QCD is a robust tool for exploring the high-density QCD phase diagram beyond current lattice capabilities. Future experiments should focus on signatures like in-medium modifications of hadrons and collective flow to validate the predicted phases and transition mechanisms.

title: The ocean worlds science case for the Pollux spectropolarimeter
comment: 9 pages, 4 figures, contribution to the Pollux science case for the Habitable Worlds Observatory (HWO)
summary: Pollux is a candidate European instrument contribution to the Habitable Worlds Observatory (HWO), designed to advance our understanding of the formation and evolution of cosmic structures in the universe, and specifically search signs of life on extrasolar planets. This high-resolution spectrograph (R\,$>$\,40,000) with polarimetric capabilities offers nearly continuous and simultaneous coverage from the FUV ($\sim$100\,nm) to the NIR ($\sim$1.9\,$\micron$), making it a versatile tool for a wide range of scientific investigations from solar system studies to cosmology. Several Solar System ocean worlds have been the focal point of the scientific community to understand the conditions of their internal saline oceans, as well as the possible emergence of life beyond Earth. The ocean world science case will leverage Pollux's UV spectropolarimetric capabilities to investigate surface reflectance and composition, characterize airglow emissions in the environments of giant-planet moons, as well as constrain the microphysical properties of atmospheric aerosols.

AI.tldr: Pollux is a high-resolution spectrograph for the Habitable Worlds Observatory (HWO) that will study cosmic structures, search for life on exoplanets, and investigate Solar System ocean worlds using UV spectropolarimetry to analyze surface composition, airglow emissions, and aerosol properties.
AI.motivation: To advance understanding of cosmic structure formation, identify life beyond Earth, and explore conditions on ocean worlds with saline oceans, which are prime targets for habitability research.
AI.method: Utilizes a high-resolution (R>40,000) spectrograph with polarimetric capabilities covering wavelengths from FUV (100 nm) to NIR (1.9 μm). Specifically employs UV spectropolarimetry to analyze surface reflectance, airglow emissions, and aerosol microphysics on ocean worlds like giant-planet moons.
AI.result: Expected to provide detailed compositional data on ocean worlds, characterize atmospheric processes, and constrain the habitability potential of extraterrestrial environments through unique spectral and polarimetric observations.
AI.conclusion: Pollux will significantly enhance exoplanet and Solar System research, enabling breakthroughs in detecting biosignatures and understanding the evolution of cosmic environments capable of supporting life.

title: Not Where You Left Them: Displaced $γ$-Rays and X-Rays Reveal the Cosmic Ray Scattering Rate
comment: 20 pages, 11 figures, 1 table, Submitted to The Astrophysical Journal (ApJ), Comments are welcome
summary: Modern X-ray and $γ$-ray instruments are revealing a growing class of Galactic non-thermal sources whose emission centroids are measurably offset from the nearest plausible sites of cosmic ray (CR) acceleration. Such "displaced" sources are seen in keV X-rays and TeV-PeV $γ$-rays but not in GeV $γ$-rays, have hard spectra, and are not associated with gas clumps, suggesting a leptonic origin. We develop a general framework for understanding displacement, whereby relativistic CR electrons (CRe) injected into the interstellar medium (ISM) with a strongly anisotropic pitch-angle distribution propagate a finite distance from their acceleration site before scattering processes isotropise their directions sufficiently for the emission to become visible. We use CR transport simulations to investigate under what circumstances displacement is likely, finding that it requires an initial pitch angle distribution $\lesssim 45^\circ$ wide, a line of sight broadly edge-on to the magnetic field, and that the source be measured in a waveband where emission is dominated by CRe for which the radiative-loss and pitch-angle scattering timescales are comparable. For typical Galactic conditions the latter condition is satisfied only for CRe energies $\gtrsim$ 10 TeV, explaining why displaced sources appear at X-ray and TeV but not GeV energies. We further show that, when displacement is detected, it allows a direct inference of the CRe pitch-angle scattering rate.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework explaining displaced non-thermal sources in X-ray/TeV gamma-rays by the anisotropic propagation of cosmic-ray electrons before isotropization, linking displacement to specific pitch-angle conditions and energy regimes.
AI.motivation: To address the observation of displaced non-thermal emission sources not aligned with acceleration sites, particularly their absence in GeV gamma-rays and association with hard spectra and lack of gas clumps.
AI.method: Developed a CR transport simulation model investigating conditions for source displacement considering initial pitch-angle distribution, magnetic field orientation, and energy-dependent emission dominance by cosmic-ray electrons with balanced radiative loss and scattering timescales.
AI.result: Displacement requires initial pitch-angle distribution <45°, edge-on magnetic field alignment, and occurs at energies ≥10 TeV, explaining X-ray/TeV visibility and GeV absence. Displacement enables direct inference of electron pitch-angle scattering rates.
AI.conclusion: Displaced sources arise from anisotropic CR electron propagation before scattering isotropizes them, with observable displacement only at high energies, providing a method to measure scattering rates critical for understanding cosmic-ray transport.

title: Conventional vs. modified GTD metrics: Survival of modified GTD metrics in AdS spacetime and thermodynamic ensembles
summary: Thermodynamic geometry provides a powerful framework for probing the microscopic structure of thermodynamic systems. Among its formulations, Geometrothermodynamics (GTD) has been widely applied to black hole thermodynamics, owing to its Legendre-invariant construction. However, recent work has shown that conventional GTD metrics fail to encode essential physical boundaries of thermodynamic phase space. By modifying the conventional metric structure, three new GTD metrics were previously introduced, which successfully capture these boundaries in regular spacetime. Whether such modified metrics remain viable in different spacetime backgrounds and under changes of thermodynamic ensemble has remained an open question. In this work, I address this issue by investigating the behavior of modified GTD metrics in AdS spacetime and across different thermodynamic ensembles in the framework of Bardeen AdS black hole. An analysis of thermodynamic geodesics demonstrates that the modified GTD metrics consistently respect physical boundaries of the phase space, in contrast to conventional GTD metrics. This behavior is preserved in AdS spacetime and under Legendre transformations, establishing the robustness and universality of the modified GTD framework.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates the validity of modified Geometrothermodynamics (GTD) metrics in AdS spacetime and across thermodynamic ensembles using the Bardeen AdS black hole, showing that these metrics maintain consistency with physical phase space boundaries where conventional GTD fails.
AI.motivation: To address the open question of whether modified GTD metrics, which successfully capture thermodynamic phase space boundaries in regular spacetime, remain viable in AdS spacetime and under changes in thermodynamic ensembles.
AI.method: Analyzes thermodynamic geodesics of modified GTD metrics in AdS spacetime for the Bardeen AdS black hole, comparing their behavior across different thermodynamic ensembles through Legendre transformations.
AI.result: Modified GTD metrics consistently respect physical phase space boundaries in AdS spacetime and under Legendre transformations, unlike conventional GTD metrics which fail to do so.
AI.conclusion: The modified GTD framework demonstrates robustness and universality, validating its application across diverse spacetime backgrounds and thermodynamic ensembles.

title: Intrinsic Nonlocality of Spin- and Polarization-Resolved Probabilities in Strong-Field Quantum Electrodynamics
summary: Spin and polarization are central to precision tests of fundamental physics and for interpreting radiation from astrophysical sources and ultraintense laser-matter experiments. Predictive modeling therefore requires not only energy spectra, but also angle-, spin-, and polarization-resolved particle distributions. Here, we demonstrate that a key assumption underlying current strong-field quantum electrodynamics (QED) models, i.e., that emission can be treated as an instantaneous random event sampled from a local differential rate, breaks down once emission angles, electron spin, and/or photon polarization are resolved. Namely, the resulting fully differential distribution can deviate strongly from the true result and can even yield inconsistent probabilities that take negative values. The physical reason is simple: a photon emission probability builds up over a finite length of the electron trajectory, the formation region, during which the electron direction changes by roughly the same small angle that defines the radiation cone. We therefore integrate over this formation region analytically to obtain a physically consistent electron spin and photon polarization model whose implementation is compatible with existing Monte Carlo and particle-in-cell (PIC) workflows. Simulations of a GeV-class electron-laser collision and of emission in a pulsar-like magnetic field reveal spin and polarization patterns that differ even qualitatively from state-of-the-art local models. In particular, our new model predicts substantial angle-dependent circular photon polarization where the standard approach yields none, and a pronounced helicity bias in the recoiling electrons absent from current predictions. These findings have direct implications for upcoming strong-field QED experiments and for interpreting polarized radiation from extreme astrophysical environments.

AI.tldr: This paper challenges a fundamental assumption in strong-field QED modeling by showing that treating photon emission as an instantaneous event leads to inconsistencies, such as negative probabilities, when considering angle/spin/polarization. It introduces a new model accounting for emission over a finite formation region, yielding physically accurate spin/polarization predictions for electrons and photons in high-energy scenarios.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address inaccuracies in current QED models that arise when trying to resolve emission angles, electron spin, or photon polarization. Existing 'local' models lead to unphysical results, requiring a more precise approach for modern experiments and astrophysical interpretations.
AI.method: The authors analytically integrate photon emission probabilities over the electron trajectory's formation region (where emission builds up over a finite path). This accounts for small-angle trajectory changes during radiation cone formation, ensuring probability consistency and positivity. They implemented this in Monte Carlo and PIC frameworks.
AI.result: New model produces qualitatively different predictions compared to state-of-the-art methods: significant angle-dependent circular photon polarization (absent in standard models) and pronounced electron helicity bias. Tests with GeV electron-laser collisions and pulsar magnetic fields validate these differences.
AI.conclusion: Current QED models' local approximation is insufficient for detailed angular/spin/polarization studies. The proposed formation region-based approach is compatible with existing computational tools, offering essential improvements for interpreting upcoming experiments and astrophysical observations involving polarization.

title: Masses of Potentially Habitable Planets Characterized by the Habitable Worlds Observatory
comment: 11 pages, 5 figures. Habitable Worlds Observatory (HWO) Science Case Development Document (SCDD), presented at the "Towards the Habitable Worlds Observatory: Visionary Science and Transformational Technology" conference in July 2025, and to be published in the conference proceedings
summary: Constraints on the masses of exoplanets directly imaged and characterized by the Habitable Worlds Observatory (HWO) are crucial for categorizing these planets and interpreting their spectra. In particular, achieving a mass measurement with a precision of approximately 10% or better may be necessary to identify the dominant gaseous species in the atmospheres of Earth-like planets. This is essential for assessing their habitability and interpreting potential biosignatures (arXiv:2502.01513). Space-based astrometry will be required to measure the masses of planets in face-on systems, or planets orbiting hot and rapidly rotating or highly active stars. Astrometric uncertainties are dominated by the number and magnitude of background reference stars needed to precisely measure the astrometric wobble of the target star induced by the planet. To that end, we propose a program to measure the masses of Earth analogs orbiting HWO target stars with ultra-high-precision astrometry obtained with the HWO high-resolution instrument. We assess the photon-noise error budget for these observations. We find that, for a field of view spanning a few square arcminutes, the astrometric uncertainty due to the number and brightness of reference stars dominates the photon-noise error budget, particularly for targets near the Galactic poles. We explored the impact of filter choice and location in the sky on the photon-noise astrometric uncertainties by simulating the magnitude distribution of reference stars across different filters at a range of galactic longitudes and latitudes. We find that a ~ 200-day survey in the Gaia G band consisting of 100 epochs per target star distributed over the 5-year prime mission with a 6m aperture HWO equipped with a 6' x 6' field-of-view would be required to achieve the photon-noise sensitivity to measure the masses of the ~ 40 Earth-mass habitable-zone planets to ~10%.

AI.tldr: The paper explores methods to achieve precise mass measurements of Earth-like exoplanets using the Habitable Worlds Observatory (HWO) via ultra-high-precision astrometry, focusing on photon-noise limitations and optimal survey strategies. Simulations assess reference star brightness and distribution across sky locations and filters to minimize astrometric uncertainties.
AI.motivation: Accurate mass measurements (within ~10% precision) are critical for identifying atmospheric composition, assessing habitability, and validating biosignatures of Earth-like exoplanets, especially in systems where radial velocity methods are ineffective.
AI.method: The study uses simulations to analyze photon-noise error budgets for HWO's high-resolution instrument. It evaluates how reference star counts/brightness influence astrometry, testing different sky locations (Galactic poles vs. planes), filters (Gaia G-band optimal), and observing strategies (e.g., 200-day survey with 100 epochs over 5 years).
AI.result: A 6m HWO survey in Gaia G-band over 200 days with 100 epochs can achieve ~10% mass precision for ~40 Earth-mass planets in habitable zones. Galactic pole targets face higher uncertainties due to fewer bright reference stars. Optimal filter choice and extended observation durations mitigate these challenges.
AI.conclusion: Successful mass determination of Earth analogs requires careful consideration of reference star availability in HWO's field-of-view. The proposed Gaia G-band strategy balances sensitivity and reference star density, enabling habitability assessments through precise planetary mass constraints.

title: Neutrino Spectral Pinching in 3D Core-Collapse Supernovae: Late-Time Convergence, Failed-Explosion Signatures, and Viewing-Angle Dispersion
summary:
We present a systematic survey of the neutrino spectral pinching parameter alpha_p(t, M, n-hat) across the Princeton Fornax ensemble of 3D core-collapse supernova simulations. We analyze 25 simulations spanning progenitor masses 8.1-100 M_sun with
  durations up to 8.47 s post-bounce, computed with the Fornax code and the SFHo equation of state. The pinching parameter alpha_p = (2^2 - E_rms^2)/(E_rms^2 - ^2) is derived from 12-bin spectral moments on a 128x256 sky grid for three neutrino
  species, enabling time- and angle-resolved spectral characterization.
  Four results emerge. (1) The nu-bar_e pinching floor is alpha_p = 1.92 +/- 0.10 (N=13 long-running models), lying 0.2-0.4 below 1D predictions due to 3D PNS convection. (2) Both BH-forming models (12.25, 14 M_sun) show anti-pinching (alpha_p <
  0.9) before collapse, with deficit Delta alpha_p ~ 0.65 visible from t = 0.5 s. (3) Two of six long-running models exhibit a hierarchy reversal ( > ) after t = 5 s; leptonic flavors carry (40 +/- 3)% of radiated energy. (4) The LESA dipole is
  suppressed by >3x in BH-forming models; viewing-angle spread Delta alpha_p(68%) ~ 0.8-1.5 dominates spectral-inversion uncertainty. Mollweide sky maps reveal coherent angular structures with alpha_p anticorrelated with luminosity and correlated
  with mean energy.
  Detection rates at Hyper-Kamiokande, DUNE, JUNO, and IceCube yield 8-12% NMO/IMO discrimination during Kelvin-Helmholtz cooling. The late-time nu-bar_e pinching floor represents the first 3D characterization of spectral convergence during
  Kelvin-Helmholtz cooling, lying 0.2-0.4 below 1D predictions, a direct signature of 3D PNS convective transport

AI.tldr: The paper systematically analyzes the neutrino spectral pinching parameter in 3D core-collapse supernova simulations, revealing discrepancies from 1D models and insights into black hole formation and detection potential.
AI.motivation: To understand the impact of 3D effects on neutrino spectra, particularly the pinching parameter, and assess its implications for distinguishing supernova models and detecting black hole formation.
AI.method: Analyzing 25 simulations from the Princeton Fornax ensemble using the SFHo equation of state, calculating spectral moments and alpha_p across time, angles, and neutrino species for 8.1-100 M_sun progenitors.
AI.result: Key findings include lower nu-bar_e pinching in 3D models due to PNS convection, anti-pinching in black hole-forming models, hierarchy reversals, reduced LESA dipole, and detection rate estimates for neutrino observatories.
AI.conclusion: 3D simulations show significant deviations from 1D predictions, emphasizing the role of convection in spectral features, and provide a basis for observable signatures in upcoming neutrino detectors.

title: Shadow of Bonanno-Reuter Black Hole in Plasma Medium: Insights from EHT Sgr A* Observations
comment: Accepted for publication in Nuclear Physics B
summary: We investigate the properties of black hole shadows in the renormalization group (RG) improved Bonanno-Reuter spacetime, incorporating quantum gravitational corrections via the scale-dependent parameter $(\tildeω)$ in a plasma medium. Light propagation in a non-uniform, pressureless plasma with a radial density profile is analyzed through modified equations of motion. The black hole shadow angular radius is computed, and its dependence on $\tildeω$ and the plasma index is analyzed. The analysis of specific limiting cases indicates systematic deviations of the black hole shadow relative to the classical Schwarzschild limit. Using Event Horizon Telescope (EHT) observations of Sgr~A*, we place constraints on $\tildeω$. Furthermore, within the considered parameter range, plasma and quantum-gravity effects exhibit an observational degeneracy, which future high-resolution measurements with the next-generation EHT are expected to break, thereby providing tighter constraints on the model parameters.

AI.tldr: The paper examines black hole shadow properties in an RG-improved spacetime with quantum gravity effects and plasma, showing deviations from classical Schwarzschild predictions and using EHT data to constrain parameters.
AI.motivation: To explore how quantum gravitational corrections and plasma medium affect black hole shadows, and to test the Bonanno-Reuter spacetime model against EHT observations.
AI.method: Analyzes light propagation in a plasma medium using modified equations of motion with a scale-dependent parameter $	ildeω$. Computes shadow angular radius, examines dependence on $	ildeω$ and plasma index. Uses Sgr A* EHT data to constrain parameters.
AI.result: Observed deviations from Schwarzschild shadows; plasma and quantum gravity effects show observational degeneracy that future EHT measurements could resolve.
AI.conclusion: Quantum gravity effects and plasma influence black hole shadow observations. Next-gen EHT data will break degeneracy between effects to better constrain $	ildeω$ and model parameters.

title: Dispersive Analysis of $D$- and $B$-Meson Form Factors with Chiral and Heavy-Quark Constraints
summary: We analyze the isovector vector form factors of $D$, $D^*$, $B$, and $B^*$ mesons at low energies. We employ all constraints due to chiral and heavy-quark symmetry, and include the physics of resonant pion-pion rescattering in a model-independent way, using dispersion theory. Special attention is paid to the analytic properties of these form factors, which include anomalous thresholds due to triangle diagrams that are located on the physical Riemann sheets in some of the form factors. We extract the couplings of the $ρ(770)$ resonance to all these heavy mesons by determining the appropriate pole residues.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes the vector form factors of D, D*, B, and B* mesons at low energies using dispersion theory, incorporating chiral and heavy-quark symmetries, and determines ρ(770) resonance couplings via pole residues.
AI.motivation: To understand the low-energy behavior of heavy meson form factors and extract resonance couplings, considering chiral and heavy-quark symmetries and pion-pion rescattering effects.
AI.method: Employed dispersion theory with chiral and heavy-quark symmetry constraints, included resonant pion-pion rescattering effects, analyzed anomalous thresholds from triangle diagrams, and determined ρ(770) pole residues for coupling extraction.
AI.result: Extracted ρ(770) resonance couplings to heavy mesons by analyzing form factor analytic properties and pole residues derived from dispersion relations.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully incorporates symmetry constraints and rescattering physics to model-independent coupling determination, providing insights into heavy meson interactions with vector resonances.

title: Two Point Correlation Function Estimation with Contaminated Data
summary: The two-point correlation function (2PCF) is a cornerstone of precision cosmology, yet its estimation from imaging surveys is vulnerable to contamination and incompleteness arising from imperfect target selection and pipeline-level inclusion decisions. In practice, the scientific target is a physically defined population, while the working catalog is constructed from noisy measurements and selection cuts, leading to mismatches between true and observed inclusion. These errors are often spatially structured, correlating with survey depth, observing conditions, and foregrounds, and can imprint spurious large-scale power or suppress the true clustering signal. High-resolution spectroscopic samples provide gold-standard inclusion in the target population but are typically available for only a small subset of objects. We introduce a prediction-powered Landy--Szalay (PP--LS) estimator that combines noisy inclusion labels across the full catalog with exact labels on a small spectroscopic subset while preserving the standard random-catalog normalization for survey geometry and selection. PP--LS debiases pair counts using residual-based, design-weighted corrections computed only on the labeled subset, requiring no probability calibration, known misclassification rates, or explicit modeling of contamination. Under simple random sampling of the labeled subset, we establish recovery of the oracle (true-label) Landy--Szalay pair counts and thus consistency for the target 2PCF. In simulations with clustered and spatially structured contaminants, PP--LS removes the bias of naive catalog-level estimators while achieving substantially lower variance than spectroscopic-only clustering. The resulting estimator is statistically principled, computationally lightweight, and integrates directly with standard pair-counting pipelines, enabling robust clustering inference in next-generation surveys.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the PP-LS estimator, which combines noisy inclusion labels from large catalogs and exact labels from a small spectroscopic sample to accurately estimate the two-point correlation function (2PCF) in cosmology, addressing biases from spatially structured selection effects and contamination.
AI.motivation: Imaging surveys suffer from biases in 2PCF estimation due to imperfect target selection and spatially structured contamination. Existing methods either use limited spectroscopic data (high purity but small size) or noisy photometric data (large but biased). The goal is to create a robust estimator that reduces bias and variance by leveraging both datasets.
AI.method: PP-LS estimator merges noisy photometric labels with precise spectroscopic labels. It uses residual-based, design-weighted corrections computed on the labeled subset to debias pair counts without requiring probability calibration or explicit contamination models. It preserves standard random-catalog normalization and integrates with existing pipelines.
AI.result: Simulation results show PP-LS eliminates bias from naive estimators and reduces variance compared to spectroscopic-only methods. It recovers oracle-level pair counts under random sampling assumptions, demonstrating consistency for the target 2PCF.
AI.conclusion: The PP-LS estimator provides a statistically sound, computationally efficient solution for accurate 2PCF estimation in surveys with incomplete or contaminated catalogs. It addresses key challenges of spatially varying selection effects, enabling reliable cosmological inference in future large-scale surveys.

title: PACHA: Probing AGN Coronae with High-redshift AGN
comment: 28 pages, submitted to ApJ, 13 figures, 18 tables
summary: The X-ray emission of active galactic nuclei (AGN) is generally attributed to inverse Compton scattering of accretion-disk photons by hot electrons in a compact corona. In local AGN, directly constraining coronal properties is challenging because the high-energy cutoff often lies beyond the NuSTAR bandpass. High-redshift, luminous quasars enable systematic constraints on the high-energy cutoff, as cosmological redshift shifts the spectal cutoff into the observable hard X-ray band. We present first results from the ``Probing the AGN Coronae with High-redshift AGN'' (PACHA) project, based on quasi-simultaneous NuSTAR and XMM-Newton observations of 13 radio-quiet AGN at $z>1$. We constrain the high-energy cutoff and coronal temperature at 90\% confidence level for 10 and 9 sources, respectively. The sample exhibits a mean cutoff energy of $E_{\rm cut}=80.8\pm8.1$ keV and a mean coronal temperature of $kT_{\rm e}=18.4\pm1.6$ keV, both significantly lower than those measured in local {\it Swift}-BAT AGN, while the mean optical depth ($τ=4.8\pm0.3$) is significantly higher. The uncertainties are at 1~$σ$. Combining our high-redshift sample with local AGN, we find a potential anti-correlation between cutoff energy and both X-ray luminosity and black hole mass, with no significant dependence on Eddington ratio. Within a hybrid coronal framework, the inferred temperatures lie well below the pair-production limits for purely thermal coronae, indicating a substantial efficient Compton cooling and/or non-thermal electron component. The detection of low coronal temperatures in high-luminosity AGN is broadly consistent with predictions from recent radiation MHD simulations that consider purely thermal electron populations, implying that non-thermal electrons may not be the primary drivers of the observed coronal properties in these systems.

AI.tldr: The PACHA project's study of high-redshift quasars using NuSTAR and XMM-Newton data reveals that AGN coronae at z>1 have lower cutoff energies and temperatures but higher optical depth compared to local AGN. These findings suggest efficient Compton cooling or non-thermal electron components, aligning with radiation MHD simulations that don't require non-thermal electrons as primary drivers.
AI.motivation: To systematically constrain the high-energy cutoff and coronal properties of AGN coronae at higher redshifts where cosmological effects make X-ray spectra observable with current instruments.
AI.method: Quasi-simultaneous observations of 13 high-redshift (z>1) radio-quiet AGN using NuSTAR and XMM-Newton. Statistical analysis of spectral data to derive cutoff energy, coronal temperature, and optical depth at 90% confidence. Comparison with local Swift-BAT AGN data sets.
AI.result: Sample shows mean E_cut=80.8±8.1 keV and kT_e=18.4±1.6 keV, both lower than local AGN, but τ=4.8±0.3 higher. Potential anti-correlation found between cutoff energy and X-ray luminosity/black hole mass. Temperatures below pair-production limits suggest Compton cooling or non-thermal components.
AI.conclusion: High-redshift AGN coronae differ structurally from local ones, indicating evolving coronal properties. Results support scenarios with efficient cooling rather than non-thermal electrons as dominant factors, consistent with some MHD simulations.

title: BBN to Late-Time Acceleration in $f(T,\mathcal{L}_m)$ Gravity
summary:
We present, to our knowledge, the first systematic study of early-late cosmic evolution and acceleration in the framework of $f(T,\mathcal{L}_m)$ gravity, an extension of teleparallel theories coupling torsion with the matter Lagrangian. By incorporating the Big-Bang Nucleosynthesis (BBN) bound on the freeze-out temperature, we obtain a tight constraint on the inverse-torsion parameter, ensuring consistency with early-time physics.
  Employing Markov Chain Monte Carlo analyses with progressively richer observational datasets, CC, Union3, and SN22 supernovae, we constrain a well-motivated model and reconstruct key cosmological functions. The reconstructed Hubble and distance modulus functions show excellent agreement with the observations, confirming the observational viability of the model.
  The model successfully reproduces the observed late-time expansion history, yielding a transition from deceleration to acceleration through the deceleration parameter. The effective equation of state is found to remain negative throughout, with present values $w_0 > -1$, indicating a quintessence-like behavior rather than a cosmological constant or phantom regime. These results highlight the ability of $f(T,\mathcal{L}_m)$ gravity to mimic the concordance scenario while allowing controlled deviations in the expansion history.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first systematic study of early-late cosmic evolution and acceleration in f(T,L_m) gravity, incorporating BBN constraints on the inverse-torsion parameter. Using MCMC analyses with observational data, the model successfully reproduces the late-time expansion history with a quintessence-like equation of state.
AI.motivation: To explore the cosmic evolution and acceleration in f(T,L_m) gravity extensions of teleparallel theories, ensuring consistency with early-time physics like BBN, and validate the model against observational data.
AI.method: Systematic study incorporating BBN freeze-out temperature constraints on inverse-torsion parameters. Applied MCMC analyses with CC, Union3, and SN22 supernovae datasets to constraint model parameters. Reconstructed Hubble and distance modulus functions for comparison with observations.
AI.result: Model shows excellent agreement with observational data through reconstructed cosmological functions. Demonstrates transition from deceleration to acceleration with effective equation of state w_0 > -1 (quintessence-like), avoiding phantom regime. Model mimics concordance cosmology with controlled expansion deviations.
AI.conclusion: f(T,L_m) gravity provides a viable framework compatible with both early-time constraints and late-time observations, offering a dynamical dark energy alternative with quintessence behavior without requiring exotic matter or negative kinetic terms.

title: Comprehensive Effective Field Theory Analysis for Baryon Number Violating Processes
summary: Baryon number-violating processes arise generically in many extensions of the Standard Model, with Grand Unified Theories providing the most compelling realizations. Ongoing experimental searches at JUNO, Hyper-K, and DUNE motivate a more precise and model-independent analysis utilizing the effective field theory (EFT) framework capable of connecting ultraviolet dynamics to low-energy hadronic observables. In this work, we perform a pipeline analysis that connects baryon number-violating (BNV) new physics to its low-energy description through the Standard Model EFT (SMEFT) up to dimension nine and the Low-Energy EFT (LEFT) up to dimension eight, and subsequently matches onto chiral perturbation theory using a systematic spurion method. We show that the complete set of dimension-eight LEFT operators enables the inclusion of the full set of chiral representations for three-quark operators with derivatives, which are also embedded in the chiral Lagrangian after quark-hadron matching. By contrast, the conventional dimension-six operators generate only the $(\bar{\mathbf{3}},\mathbf{3})$ and $(\mathbf{8},\mathbf{1})$ representations. The higher dimensional LEFT operators should be matched from higher order SMEFT operators, and thus a wider class of UV completions are allowed. We consider the complete tree-level UV resonances for dimension 6 and 7 SMEFT operators, and representative UVs for higher dimension operators.

AI.tldr: This paper develops an effective field theory approach to analyze baryon number-violating processes, connecting high-energy physics to low-energy observables through SMEFT and LEFT frameworks up to higher dimensions, revealing new chiral representations and broader UV completions.
AI.motivation: Investigate baryon number violation in standard model extensions using a model-independent EFT approach to link UV physics with low-energy hadronic experiments like JUNO, Hyper-K, and DUNE.
AI.method: Uses SMEFT (up to dimension 9) and LEFT (up to dimension 8), applies chiral perturbation theory with spurion method, analyzes UV completions for dimension 6-7 operators and representative cases for higher dimensions.
AI.result: Dimension-8 LEFT operators allow full chiral representations (including new $(\bar{\mathbf{3}},\mathbf{3})$, $(\mathbf{8},\mathbf{1})$ beyond conventional dimension-6) and enable broader UV models. Systematic framework connects higher-dimensional SMEFT operators to hadronic observables.
AI.conclusion: Higher-dimensional operators in SMEFT/LEFT are essential for capturing full baryon-violating dynamics, opening avenues for testing UV models via low-energy experiments.

title: Proto-NUX: A prototype telescope for ground-based near-ultraviolet observations
summary: The Near-UV-eXplorer (NUX) is a proposed ground-based, wide-field telescope array with a field of view of $\sim$70 square degrees, designed to operate over the 300-350 nm wavelength range and to achieve a target sensitivity of 20 mag in 150 seconds (5 sigma). Its main scientific objective is the detection and characterization of hot, rapidly evolving transients in the near-UV (NUV). Proto-NUX is a pathfinder instrument for NUX, based on an off-the-shelf 36 cm Celestron RASA wide-field astrograph that has been modified to enhance throughput and image quality in the targeted NUV band. The main objectives of Proto-NUX are: (1) to quantify the NUV sensitivity of the prototype and assess the feasibility of the full NUX facility; and (2) to characterize atmospheric extinction in the NUV, including its temporal variability and its dependence on zenith angle. Using three filter configurations, we aim to measure the wavelength dependence of the atmospheric extinction and to disentangle the contributions from Rayleigh scattering (dominating at wavelengths >325 nm) and molecular ozone-dominated absorption (dominating <315 nm). On-site testing is scheduled for 2026 at the Pic du Midi Observatory (France, 2877 m altitude) in order to evaluate on-sky performance under high-altitude observing conditions.

AI.tldr: The Proto-NUX is a prototype telescope designed to test the feasibility of the larger NUX telescope array by measuring NUV sensitivity and atmospheric extinction characteristics.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to assess the potential of the NUX project through a precursor instrument and to understand atmospheric effects in the near-UV band critical for planning the future array.
AI.method: Proto-NUX modifies a commercial telescope to observe in the 300-350 nm range with three filter configurations to separate Rayleigh scattering and ozone absorption effects, with on-site tests planned at Pic du Midi Observatory.
AI.result: Expected outcomes include quantified NUV sensitivity of the prototype, characterized atmospheric extinction variability, and validation of the NUX concept.
AI.conclusion: Successful Proto-NUX testing will demonstrate feasibility for the NUX project and provide critical atmospheric data for optimizing the final telescope array design.

title: Imploding Remnants: detection bias against AGNs in massive clusters
comment: 13 pages, 6 figures; accepted in the Journal of High Energy Astrophysics
summary: We propose that an observed scarcity of remnant lobed AGNs in dense clusters results from a peculiarity in their dynamics upon the cessation of jet activity: a rapid `implosion' of lobes that, in their active phase, were primarily supported by the momentum flux of the jet. We investigate this behaviour by analysing the asymptotic behaviour of the RAiSE dynamical model and comparing our predictions both to the full model and hydrodynamic simulations. We find that remnant lobes powered by weak jets in massive clusters are unstable to implosion on the order of at most a few Myr. Consequently, remnant AGNs in massive clusters ($M_\text{halo} \sim 10^{14.5}$~M$_\odot$) will be under-counted by a factor of at least five compared to those in poorer groups ($M_\text{halo} \sim 10^{12}$~M$_\odot$). The lack of such remnants in observed populations may lead to a significant underestimate of the AGN feedback provided by low-powered jets, especially given their prevalence towards cluster cores where feedback is most effective. We discuss the influence of a stabilising magnetic field sheath on the nature of the implosion: does the lobe cleanly implode in on itself, or do fluid instabilities turbulently mix the lobe and ambient medium?

AI.tldr: The study explains that remnant lobed AGNs are rarely seen in dense clusters due to rapid lobe implosion after jet cessation, leading to underestimation of AGN feedback from low-powered jets.
AI.motivation: To address the observed scarcity of remnant lobed AGNs in dense clusters and understand the impact of jet cessation dynamics on their visibility and feedback contribution.
AI.method: Analyses the asymptotic behavior of the RAiSE dynamical model, compares predictions with full model simulations, and examines the role of magnetic fields in implosion stability.
AI.result: Remnant lobes powered by weak jets in massive clusters implode within ~ few million years, leading to a fivefold undercount in massive clusters compared to poorer groups.
AI.conclusion: The rapid implosion explains the lack of observed remnants, indicating AGN feedback from low-powered jets is underestimated, especially in cluster cores where feedback is most critical.

title: High post-Minkowskian gravitational waveform for hyperbolic encounters in the extreme-mass-ratio limit
summary: The frequency-domain waveform emitted by a two-body scattering process is computed in the extreme-mass-ratio limit through the fifth post-Minkowskian (PM) order (i.e., $O(G^5)$) and the fractional sixth post-Newtonian (PN) order. The current accuracy of the scattering waveform obtained by quantum amplitude methods is the one-loop level corresponding to the 3PM order, whereas the 4PM waveform is known up to the 2PN order only as derived within the traditional multipolar-post-Minkowskian formalism. Direct comparison between these waveforms to the first order in the mass ratio shows that they differ at most by the effect of an angular-independent time shift, leading to a complete physical agreement at the same level of accuracy. The new results at the 4PM and 5PM orders thus provide a benchmark for future multiloop calculations. The soft limit of the waveform at the leading order in the small-frequency expansion gives the gravitational wave memory, which is evaluated at the 4PM and 5PM orders. The waveform is also used to obtain the radiated energy at $O(G^6)$, improving its knowledge from 3PN to 6PN level.

AI.tldr: The paper computes the gravitational-wave emitted by a two-body scattering process in the extreme-mass-ratio limit up to the 5PM order and fractional sixth PN order, showing consistency between different methods and advancing the understanding of energy radiation and gravitational wave memory.
AI.motivation: To bridge gaps in gravitational waveform calculations by extending accuracy to higher PM/PN orders and validating methods through comparison, thereby setting benchmarks for future loop calculations and improving radiation-energy estimates.
AI.method: Combines quantum amplitude techniques and traditional multipolar-post-Minkowskian formalisms. Scalar perturbation theory is used for the extreme-mass-ratio scenario, and the waveform is analyzed in the soft-frequency limit to derive gravitational wave memory effects.
AI.result: The 4PM/5PM waveforms match previous results within time-shift ambiguities, confirming physical consistency. The radiated energy is extended from 3PN to 6PN precision, and gravitational wave memory is calculated to higher PM orders.
AI.conclusion: Successful high-order waveform calculations demonstrate compatibility between methods, reinforce the use of quantum field theory tools in gravitational physics, and provide critical data for validating future multiloop theories.

title: Machine-Learning-Inspired SMEFT Simplified Template Cross Sections: A Case Study in ZH Production
summary: The Simplified Template Cross Section (STXS) program has become the standard interface between Higgs measurements and global fits, but its fixed one-dimensional boundaries are not guaranteed to align with the phase-space directions to which the Standard Model Effective Field Theory (SMEFT) is most sensitive. We propose a machine-learning-inspired extension of STXS in which supervised classifiers are used only at the design stage to identify simple, publishable phase-space boundaries.
