Using associated Higgs production, $pp \to ZH$, as a case study and a benchmark momentum-dependent bosonic SMEFT deformation, we show that the relevant signal-background separation is well captured by a linear boundary in the $(p_T^Z,mZH)$ plane. We construct such boundaries with a linear support vector machine and with a deep-neural-network-assisted distillation procedure, and compare them directly with the standard STXS $p_T^Z$ bins through a common single-region Asimov-significance analysis. In this proof-of-concept setup, the ML-inspired regions systematically outperform the corresponding STXS regions, with the largest gains appearing in the boosted regime where SMEFT effects are concentrated. The final observable remains a simple linear cut, preserving the transparency and experimental portability that make STXS useful.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an ML-enhanced extension of the STXS framework using supervised classifiers to design optimal phase-space boundaries, demonstrated through ZH production analysis, showing improved sensitivity to SMEFT effects.
AI.motivation: The standard STXS approach uses fixed 1D boundaries that may not align optimally with phase-space regions sensitive to SMEFT deformations, limiting its ability to detect BSM signals.
AI.method: 1. Apply SVM and DNN-based distillation to identify optimal linear boundaries in (pT^Z, mZH) plane. 2. Compare new regions against traditional STXS pt^Z bins using Asimov significance analysis.
AI.result: ML-designed regions outperform STXS bins, especially in boosted regimes where SMEFT effects are strongest. Best performance comes from DNN-assisted boundaries.
AI.conclusion: ML-guided STXS extensions maintain experimental usability while improving sensitivity. This indicates machine learning can enhance traditional analysis frameworks without compromising interpretability.

title: Exploring the Viability of Fisher Discriminants in Galaxy Morphology Classification
comment: 19 pages, 5 figures, this is the English translation of the paper published in Malay
summary: One of the major challenges in astronomy involves accurately classifying galaxies, particularly distinguishing between different galaxy types. While many complex algorithms have shown strong performance in classification tasks, their complexity often results in longer processing times and increased difficulty in understanding. This study addresses this issue by exploring the viability of Fisher discriminants, a much simpler algorithm, in performing galaxy morphology classification. We tested four machine learning algorithms: the Fisher discriminant, Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs), Boosted Decision Trees (BDTs), and k-Nearest Neighbours (kNNs) to classify galaxies by the shape of their central bulges. Using data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS), we utilised five pre-processing transformations: normalisation, decorrelation, principal component analysis (PCA), uniformisation, and Gaussianisation, and classified the shape of central bulge into either rounded or no-bulge, based on the Galaxy Zoo Decision Tree. When compared to the Galaxy Zoo 2 (GZ2) labels, the Fisher discriminant with uniformisation obtained the highest accuracy score of 0.9310, outperforming ANN, BDT, and kNN by 1.93%, 0.42%, and 3.08%, respectively.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates the effectiveness of Fisher discriminants and other machine learning algorithms in classifying galaxy bulge shapes, finding Fisher with uniformisation achieves the highest accuracy (0.9310) compared to ANN, BDT, and kNN by using SDSS data and various preprocessing methods.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of accurately and efficiently classifying galaxy morphologies while maintaining interpretability, by evaluating simpler algorithms like Fisher discriminants against complex ones.
AI.method: Four algorithms (Fisher discriminant, ANN, BDT, kNN) were tested on SDSS data with five preprocessing transformations (normalisation, decorrelation, PCA, uniformisation, Gaussianisation). Classification was done on central bulge shapes (rounded/no-bulge) using GZ2 labels.
AI.result: Fisher discriminant with uniformisation achieved the highest accuracy (0.9310), outperforming ANN by 1.93%, BDT by 0.42%, and kNN by 3.08%.
AI.conclusion: Fisher discriminant combined with uniformisation provides a simpler, faster alternative to complex models for galaxy classification tasks without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Marginally stable nuclear burning triggered at different depths of the neutron star surface in Low-mass X-ray binary 4U 1608-52
comment: 12 pages, 9 figures, received the 2nd referee report from ApJ
summary: We investigated the timing and spectral properties of the millihertz quasi-periodic oscillations (mHz QPOs) in the neutron-star low-mass X-ray binary 4U 1608-52 using NICER observations. Our analysis reveals a correlation between the QPO frequency and its absolute amplitude, as well as between the frequency and the temperature of the burning layer. Intensity-resolved spectral analysis indicates that the flux modulation of the mHz QPOs is primarily caused by the variations in the blackbody temperature in most observations. Furthermore, for the first time, we report that as the source evolves from the soft spectral state toward the transitional state, the marginally stable burning responsible for the mHz QPOs ignites at deeper layers of the neutron-star surface. The radiation flux associated with the mHz QPOs shows a decreasing trend as the source moves into the transitional state. These two findings support a scenario in which the marginally stable nuclear burning ignites at deeper layers as the temperature decreases, releasing less energy from the nuclear reaction. Finally, we determine that the energy release rate of the marginally stable burning is around 10$^{35}$ erg/s, consistent with the theoretical predictions.

AI.tldr: The study of mHz QPOs in neutron-star low-mass X-ray binary 4U 1608-52 using NICER data reveals correlations between QPO frequency, amplitude, and burning layer temperature, suggesting stable nuclear burning occurs at deeper layers with cooling, consistent with theoretical predictions.
AI.motivation: To understand the physical mechanisms behind mHz QPOs in 4U 1608-52 and validate theoretical models of marginally stable nuclear burning on neutron stars.
AI.method: Analysis of NICER observations focusing on timing and spectral properties of mHz QPOs, including correlations between frequency/amplitude, frequency/temperature, and layer depth during source state transitions from soft to transitional states.
AI.result: Observed correlations between QPO parameters and burning layer conditions; first detection of deeper ignition layers during transitional state evolution; reduced radiation flux in later states; energy release rate ~10^35 erg/s.
AI.conclusion: Findings support the scenario that cooling shifts marginally stable burning to deeper layers, reducing energy release, aligning with theoretical expectations.

title: A Brief Study of Dark Energy Accretion onto Schwarzschild Black Hole : Biswas-Roy-Biswas Type Redshift Parameterization is Chosen
summary: In this letter, we have considered accretion of a particular type of Dark Energy model onto a Schwarzschild type black hole. Before using the model, the free parameters of the Dark Energy model have been constrained with differential ages data. A narrow peak on top of a wide plateau in two parameters' distributions indicates a well defined best fit value embedded within a broad region of near-degenerate solutions. This means the data strongly favours one specific parameter value but also permit a wide range with comparable likelihood. Physically, it reflects that the Dark Energy dynamics are locally constrained yet globally insensitive to small parameter variations. An increasing $\log_{10}\left[M(z)/M_{0}\right]$ since $z=3$ signifies that black holes have continuously grown through accretion and mergers within the standard hierarchical formation scenario. The precise rate of this growth depends on the radiative efficiency $ε$, the effective accretion parameter $λ_{\rm eff}$, and the cumulative impact of merger events.

AI.tldr: The study examines Dark Energy accretion onto a Schwarzschild black hole, using constrained model parameters from differential ages data. It finds a narrow peak on a wide plateau in parameter distributions, indicating a preferred parameter value with allowable variations. Black hole growth since redshift 3 is linked to accretion, mergers, and model parameters like radiative efficiency and accretion rates.
AI.motivation: To understand how Dark Energy interacts with black hole growth through accretion processes, constrained by observational data to validate the model's parameters and assess the impact of Dark Energy dynamics on cosmological structure formation.
AI.method: The researchers constrained Dark Energy model parameters using differential ages data, analyzed parameter distributions showing a narrow peak over a plateau, and modeled black hole growth from z=3 to present considering accretion, mergers, and model-dependent factors like ε and λ_eff.
AI.result: Parameter constraints revealed a preferred best-fit value within a broad allowed range, showing local sensitivity but global insensitivity. Black holes have grown steadily since z=3, with growth rates dependent on radiative efficiency, accretion parameters, and merger contributions.
AI.conclusion: Dark Energy dynamics are locally constrained but globally flexible, suggesting robustness in cosmological models. Black hole growth via accretion/mergers aligns with hierarchical structure formation, emphasizing the role of model parameters in quantifying growth rates.

title: Vector Higgs-Portal Dark Matter: How UV Completion Reopens Viable Parameter Space
comment: 15 pages and 6 figures. Comments are most welcome
summary: The particle nature of dark matter (DM) remains one of the central open problems in modern physics. Among the most extensively studied candidates are weakly interacting massive particles, whose parameter space is now under strong pressure from direct detection, indirect detection, and collider searches. In this work we revisit the Higgs-portal scenario with vector DM, first in an effective-field-theory description and then in a renormalizable UV-complete realization. We show that the effective Higgs-portal model with a Proca vector coupled quadratically to the Standard Model Higgs is essentially excluded over almost all of its parameter space by current direct-detection limits, with only a narrow region near the Higgs resonance surviving with a required fine tuning of the DM to Higgs mass that should at the permille level. We then consider a UV completion based on an additional gauged $U(1)_X$ symmetry, in which the DM candidate is a massive vector boson $V$ and the scalar sector is extended by a dark Higgs that mixes with the Standard Model Higgs. In this framework, the presence of a second scalar mediator opens an additional resonant annihilation channel and can substantially weaken the direct-detection constraints. In particular, when the DM mass lies sufficiently close to the heavy-scalar resonance, $m_V \simeq m_{H_2}/2$, the coupling required to reproduce the observed relic abundance can lie up to about two orders of magnitude below current direct-detection bounds, thereby opening viable parameter space that is absent in the effective description. Our results highlight the importance of going beyond the effective-field-theory approximation in Higgs-portal vector DM models and show that UV-complete realizations can qualitatively change the phenomenological conclusions.

AI.tldr: This paper explores Higgs-portal vector dark matter models, showing that the effective theory is mostly excluded by direct detection limits except near the Higgs resonance. A UV-complete model with a dark Higgs introduces a new resonance channel, allowing viable parameter space two orders of magnitude below current constraints.
AI.motivation: To investigate the viability of Higgs-portal vector dark matter candidates under current experimental pressures and demonstrate the necessity of UV-completion beyond effective field theory approximations.
AI.method: The study first analyzes an effective-field-theory (EFT) model with a Proca vector coupled to the Higgs, then extends it to a renormalizable UV-completion with a gauged $U(1)_X$ symmetry and a dark Higgs scalar mixing with the Standard Model Higgs.
AI.result: The EFT model is largely excluded except in a finely-tuned resonance region. The UV-complete model's additional scalar mediator opens a resonance annihilation channel, permitting DM masses near $m_{H_2}/2$ to evade direct detection limits, expanding viable parameter space by two orders of magnitude.
AI.conclusion: UV-complete Higgs-portal vector DM models provide qualitatively different phenomenology compared to EFT descriptions, highlighting the importance of incorporating full UV frameworks for accurate parameter space exploration.

title: AS-Bridge: A Bidirectional Generative Framework Bridging Next-Generation Astronomical Surveys
comment: 10 pages, 4 figures. Code available at https://github.com/ZHANG7DC/AS-Bridge
summary: The upcoming decade of observational cosmology will be shaped by large sky surveys, such as the ground-based LSST at the Vera C. Rubin Observatory and the space-based Euclid mission. While they promise an unprecedented view of the Universe across depth, resolution, and wavelength, their differences in observational modality, sky coverage, point-spread function, and scanning cadence make joint analysis beneficial, but also challenging. To facilitate joint analysis, we introduce A(stronomical)S(urvey)-Bridge, a bidirectional generative model that translates between ground- and space-based observations. AS-Bridge learns a diffusion model that employs a stochastic Brownian Bridge process between the LSST and Euclid observations. The two surveys have overlapping sky regions, where we can explicitly model the conditional probabilistic distribution between them. We show that this formulation enables new scientific capabilities beyond single-survey analysis, including faithful probabilistic predictions of missing survey observations and inter-survey detection of rare events. These results establish the feasibility of inter-survey generative modeling. AS-Bridge is therefore well-positioned to serve as a complementary component of future LSST-Euclid joint data pipelines, enhancing the scientific return once data from both surveys become available. Data and code are available at \href{https://github.com/ZHANG7DC/AS-Bridge}{https://github.com/ZHANG7DC/AS-Bridge}.

AI.tldr: AS-Bridge is a bidirectional generative model designed to translate between ground-based LSST and space-based Euclid observations, enabling joint analysis of these cosmological surveys through a diffusion-based Brownian Bridge process. It addresses challenges in integrating their differing observational parameters and demonstrates capabilities like predicting missing data and detecting rare events.
AI.motivation: The upcoming LSST and Euclid surveys offer vast observational data but present challenges due to differences in observational modalities, sky coverage, and other parameters. Joint analysis is needed to maximize scientific insights, yet current methods struggle with these discrepancies.
AI.method: AS-Bridge employs a diffusion model using a stochastic Brownian Bridge process to model the probabilistic relationship between LSST and Euclid observations. By leveraging overlapping sky regions, it learns conditional distributions for bidirectional translation between the surveys.
AI.result: The model successfully enables probabilistic predictions of missing observations and detects rare events across surveys. It proves the feasibility of inter-survey generative modeling, showing potential to enhance future joint data analysis pipelines.
AI.conclusion: AS-Bridge serves as a foundational tool for integrating LSST and Euclid data, poised to increase scientific outcomes when their datasets are combined. Its open-source implementation supports ongoing research and application in cosmological studies.

title: Numerical Simulations of the Circularized Accretion Flow in Population III Star Tidal Disruption Events. II. Radiative Properties
summary: Tidal Disruption Events (TDEs) release enormous amounts of energy, offering a promising avenue for detecting Population III (Pop III) stars. However, the radiative properties of TDEs of Pop III stars have so far been studied only analytically, relying on many assumptions. Based on our radiative hydrodynamic simulations that follow the evolution of the accretion system for Pop III star TDEs where a $300\ M_{\odot}$ ($M_{\odot}$ is the solar mass) star is disrupted by a $10^{6}\ M_{\odot}$ black hole (BH), we compute the emission properties of the event in rest frame and find that the spectrum peaks in the optical/UV waveband. After accounting for redshift ($z \sim 10$) and extinction effects, we find the observed spectral peak shifts to the infrared, with fluxes exceeding $10^{2}\mathrm{nJy}$-making such events detectable with both the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) and the Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope (Roman). The dependence of the observed spectrum on viewing angle is suppressed due to dust extinction. Using our simulation results, we also calculate the radio emission generated by the interaction between the wind and the circumnuclear medium (CNM) and find that a Pop III star TDE can produce an unusually long-lasting, continuously increasing radio flare with a duration greater than $10^4$ days and thus has the potential to be detected in radio wavebands. These results may be helpful to the detection of Pop III stars.

AI.tldr: The study uses radiative hydrodynamic simulations to analyze Tidal Disruption Events (TDEs) involving Population III (Pop III) stars, showing that such events emit detectable infrared and radio signals observable by JWST and Roman telescopes.
AI.motivation: To overcome previous analytical studies' limitations on Pop III TDE radiative properties and assess detectability via advanced telescopes.
AI.method: Radiative hydrodynamic simulations of a 300 M☉ Pop III star disrupted by a 1e6 M☉ black hole, including redshift (z ~10) and dust extinction effects on spectral observations, plus radio emission modeling from wind-CNM interactions.
AI.result: Optical/UV spectral peak shifts to infrared post-extinction (flux >100 nJy), detectable by JWST/Roman; radio flares lasting >10^4 days are predicted due to wind-CNM interaction.
AI.conclusion: Pop III TDEs are viable detection targets for upcoming telescopes via their infrared and long-duration radio signatures.

title: Evaluation of circular orbits and innermost stable circular orbits of neutral and charged particles around black holes
summary: In this paper we study the effective gravitational potential of Schwarzschild, Kerr, Reissner-Nordström and Kerr-Newman black holes with the relativistic corrections to evaluate the circular orbits and the Innermost Stable Circular Orbits (ISCOs), a purely relativistic phenomenon, of neutral and charged particles in the vicinity of these spacetimes. We study the circular orbits and ISCOs of black holes mathematically and graphically. Moreover, the astrophysical properties of ISCO are also dealt with. We find that the particle entering the ISCO loses a certain amount of energy, in different spacetimes, by gravitational radiative processes, before finally spiraling into ISCO. The electromagnetic effects of different spacetimes show how the charges sharpen the radius of the circular orbits, and the increasing product of particle and black hole charge increases the radius of the ISCO. Most importantly, the effective potential of the most general spacetime, as predicted by the no-hair conjecture, has been derived.

AI.tldr: The paper examines the effective gravitational potential of various black hole spacetimes (Schwarzschild, Kerr, Reissner-Nordström, Kerr-Newman) to analyze circular orbits and Innermost Stable Circular Orbits (ISCOs) for neutral/charged particles. It shows that charge affects ISCO radii, with increasing charge products expanding the ISCO radius, and derives the effective potential for the most general black hole under the no-hair theorem.
AI.motivation: To understand relativistic effects on orbital dynamics around black holes, particularly how charge interactions influence stable orbits and energy loss via gravitational radiation prior to final infall.
AI.method: Mathematical derivation and graphical analysis of effective potentials for different black hole spacetimes. Calculations include relativistic corrections and electromagnetic effects on particle orbits.
AI.result: Charge sharpens circular orbit radii and increases ISCO radius with higher charge product between particle and black hole. The study confirms energy loss via gravitational radiation before ISCO infall and establishes an effective potential model aligning with the no-hair conjecture.
AI.conclusion: Electromagnetic interactions significantly alter orbital stability parameters in charged black hole systems. The derived effective potential supports the no-hair theorem by encapsulating all black hole properties into mass, spin, and charge.

title: Comprehensive Mass Predictions: From Triply Heavy Baryons to Pentaquarks
summary: In this article, we use two different methods for studying the mass spectra of fully-heavy baryons and pentaquarks. In the first section, we use state-of-the-art machine learning methods, such as deep neural networks and the Particle Transformer model architecture, to predict baryon masses directly from their quantum numbers, based on experimental information on hadrons from the Particle Data Group (PDG). We use this data-driven approach for the case of fully heavy baryons, and a large number of exotic pentaquark states, going much beyond the well-known $ P_c^+(4380) $ and $ P_c^+(4457) candidates. Subsequently,we extend the Gürsey-Radicati mass formula to incorporate the contributions of charm and bottom quarks, enabling analytical calculations for both ground and radially excited states of baryons and pentaquarks. The results obtained from both approaches demonstrate strong agreement with experimental data where available and make predictions for a number of unobserved states, including higher radial excitations. By addressing the question through both data-driven prediction and analytical modeling in different frameworks, this study offers complementary insights into the mass spectrum of conventional and exotic hadrons, guiding future experimental searches.

AI.tldr: The study uses machine learning methods (deep neural networks and Particle Transformer) and an extended Gürsey-Radicati mass formula to predict mass spectra of fully-heavy baryons and exotic pentaquarks, showing agreement with existing data and predicting unobserved states.
AI.motivation: To explore the mass spectra of fully-heavy baryons and pentaquarks beyond known states like Pc+(4380) and Pc+(4457), leveraging both data-driven and analytical methods for comprehensive insights.
AI.method: 1. Machine learning approach: Trains DNNs and Particle Transformer on PDG data to predict masses from quantum numbers. 2. Extended Gürsey-Radicati formula incorporating charm/bottom quarks for analytical mass calculations of ground and excited states.
AI.result: Strong agreement with experimental data for known states; predictions for unobserved higher excitations and exotic pentaquarks through both approaches.
AI.conclusion: Complementary strengths of ML-based predictions and analytical models provide robust insights, guiding future experiments to verify predicted hadronic states.

title: 50-250 MHz Pulsar Census with an SKA-Low prototype station: Spectra and Polarization
summary: Low-frequency pulsar observations are crucial for understanding pulsar emission spectra and population physics, as well as for probing the interstellar medium (ISM) and Earth's ionosphere. We report the largest low-frequency pulsar census conducted in the southern hemisphere, covering 50-250 MHz, using the EDA2, an SKA-Low prototype station. In this survey, we detected 120 pulsars, including 23 first-time detections below 150 MHz and 5 below 100 MHz. For each source, we provide integrated pulse profiles and flux-density measurements across five sub-bands spanning 50-250 MHz. We also obtained improved dispersion measure (DM) values for 110 pulsars, with a median absolute DM correction of about 0.1 pc cm^-3. We measured significant Faraday rotation for 40 pulsars with improved rotation measure (RM) values for 4 pulsars, as well as phase-resolved RM variation in J1453-6413. Full-polarimetric pulse profiles are provided for all these pulsars, with multi-frequency polarimetric data for 20 of them. These results will enhance future SKA-Low science: refining pulsar population models, informing survey strategies, and advancing characterization of both the ISM and the ionosphere through low-frequency pulsar monitoring.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a large low-frequency pulsar survey using EDA2 in the southern hemisphere, detecting 120 pulsars with new data on emission spectra and ISM/ionosphere interactions.
AI.motivation: To enhance understanding of pulsar emission spectra, pulsar population physics, and to probe the interstellar medium and Earth's ionosphere through low-frequency observations.
AI.method: Conducted a survey at 50-250 MHz using the EDA2 prototype station, obtaining integrated pulse profiles, flux-density measurements across five sub-bands, improved DM values, RM measurements, and polarimetric data.
AI.result: Detected 120 pulsars (including first-time detections below 150/100 MHz), refined DM/RM values for many sources, observed phase-resolved RM variation in one pulsar, and provided polarimetric profiles.
AI.conclusion: These results aid future SKA-Low missions by improving pulsar population models, guiding survey strategies, and advancing analysis of the ISM and ionospheric effects via low-frequency monitoring.

title: Three-dimensional Global Relativistic Radiation Magnetohydrodynamics of Magnetically Arrested Disk Accretion Flows in AGNs
comment: 23 pages, 12 figures, 2 tables, Accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: We perform three-dimensional radiation-relativistic magnetohydrodynamic (3D Rad-RMHD) simulations of accretion flows around spinning active galactic nuclei (AGNs). Our study focuses on the magnetically arrested disk (MAD) state, adopting a single-temperature model that includes bremsstrahlung opacity as the sole radiation process while varying the black hole spin from non-spinning to rapidly spinning cases. We find that the MAD state persists across all spin values, as demonstrated by the normalized magnetic flux at the horizon and the physically motivated spatially averaged plasma beta. The overall flow dynamics remain qualitatively similar for all spin models in 3D flow, suggesting that black hole spin has minimal influence on the accretion dynamics. In addition, we conduct post-processing using a two-temperature model to calculate the luminosities from synchrotron and bremsstrahlung radiation. We find that the total radiation luminosity is significantly higher than the luminosities from synchrotron and bremsstrahlung. This finding highlights the influence of radiation on the dynamics of the accretion flow. Our analysis shows that the electron temperature is significantly high in the jet region, regardless of spin. We further find that the temporal evolution of both radiative and synchrotron luminosities exhibits qualitatively similar behavior across all spin values. Finally, our results indicate that black hole spin has minimal impact on the spectral energy distribution (SED) in MAD state accretion flows.

AI.tldr: 3D Rad-RMHD simulations of accretion flows around spinning AGNs in the MAD state reveal that black hole spin has minimal influence on accretion dynamics, plasma properties, and SED, though radiation plays a significant role in flow dynamics.
AI.motivation: To investigate how black hole spin affects accretion dynamics, plasma properties, and radiation processes in magnetically arrested disks (MAD) around AGNs.
AI.method: Used 3D radiation-relativistic magnetohydrodynamic simulations with a single-temperature model incorporating bremsstrahlung opacity. Tested varying black hole spin parameters and post-processed with a two-temperature model for luminosity calculations.
AI.result: MAD state persists across all spin values; similar flow dynamics, high electron temperatures in jets, and comparable radiative/synchrotron luminosity behaviors regardless of spin. Total radiation luminosity exceeds synchrotron/bremsstrahlung contributions, emphasizing radiation's dynamical role.
AI.conclusion: Black hole spin has minimal impact on accretion dynamics, plasma beta, jet electron temperatures, or SED in MAD states. Radiation processes significantly influence flow dynamics despite spin variations.

title: Analytic Singular Slow-roll Inflation
summary: We study a class of minimally coupled scalar field theories which leads to analytic solutions for the Hubble rate and the scalar field. The inflationary phenomenology for this class of models can be studied fully analytically. The resulting phenomenology is compatible with the ACT data and for limiting cases, the spectral index is bluer than the ACT constraints and tends to the value $n_{\mathcal{S}}=0.98$, while in the limiting case, the tensor-to-scalar ratio takes very small values, nearly zero. More importantly, the resulting cosmology describes a Universe that has a finite scale factor at $t=0$, thus non-singular, evolves and expands realizing a slow-roll inflationary era and after that it reaches classically a pressure singularity. Classically, the Universe can pass through this singularity, and a turnaround cosmology is realized with the Universe contracting after the turnaround point. However, before the singularity is realized classically, the quantum phenomena dominate the evolution, avoiding the singularity. Specifically we consider the Nojiri-Odintsov conformal anomaly mechanism and we prove that the conformal anomaly erases the classical singular evolution and at the same time it generates extreme particle creation, which eventually reheats the Universe. Thus in this model the scalar field oscillations and the numerous couplings of the inflaton to the Standard Model particles are not required for reheating. In this context, scalar perturbations are enhanced and thus the formation of primordial black holes and the generation of secondary gravitational waves is enhanced. We also discuss several other mechanisms that may lead to the avoidance of the pressure singularity.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a scalar field theory model of inflation leading to analytical solutions for the Hubble rate and scalar field. It describes a nonsingular universe evolving through slow-roll inflation, followed by a pressure singularity resolved by quantum effects (conformal anomaly) which induces reheating through extreme particle creation, eliminating the need for inflaton oscillations or particle couplings. Enhanced scalar perturbations promote primordial black hole formation and secondary gravitational waves.
AI.motivation: To explore nonsingular cosmological models that resolve classical singularities via quantum effects and to provide an analytical framework for inflationary phenomenology compatible with ACT data, while addressing reheating and primordial black hole formation without standard inflaton decay mechanisms.
AI.method: Analytical derivation of minimally coupled scalar field models leading to exact solutions, incorporating the Nojiri-Odintsov conformal anomaly mechanism to resolve pressure singularities. Analysis of resulting spectral index (nearing n_s=0.98) and tensor-to-scalar ratio (approaching zero) against ACT data constraints.
AI.result: The model demonstrates a nonsingular universe evolution with quantum effects replacing classical singularities, successful analytical matching to observational data, and enhanced primordial black hole/gravitational wave production without traditional reheating processes.
AI.conclusion: Conformal anomaly mechanisms effectively eliminate classical cosmological singularities, provide a viable inflationary framework matching observations, and offer new avenues for reheating and structure formation through quantum-induced particle creation and enhanced perturbations.

title: Quantum tomography of $H \to ZZ, WW$ beyond leading order
summary: We revisit quantum tomography of $H \to ZZ$ and $H \to WW$ in the presence of higher-order corrections. We verify that neither the use of an effective spin analysing power (only for $ZZ$) or a photon veto are sufficient to render the naively-constructed spin density operators physical. A subtraction of higher-order corrections is thus necessary to perform consistent quantum tomography. Such corrections are small when compared to expected experimental uncertainties with current data. As a by-product, we point out the striking possibility to observe parity-violating effects in $H \to WW$.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates quantum tomography for H→ZZ and H→WW processes considering higher-order corrections, finding that naive methods fail to ensure physical results, necessitating corrections subtraction. It also highlights parity-violating effects in H→WW.
AI.motivation: To address the shortcomings of existing methods in quantum tomography for H→ZZ and H→WW when higher-order corrections are present, ensuring physically valid results and exploring potential parity-violating effects.
AI.method: Revisiting quantum tomography by incorporating higher-order corrections, evaluating the adequacy of effective spin analysing power and photon veto techniques, and analyzing resultant corrections' impact on experimental uncertainties.
AI.result: Higher-order corrections must be subtracted to achieve physical density matrices; these corrections are smaller than current experimental uncertainties. Parity-violating effects in H→WW are potentially observable.
AI.conclusion: Subtraction of higher-order corrections is essential for accurate quantum tomography in these processes. The discovery of parity violation offers new experimental opportunities.

title: Assessing the robustness of amortized simulation-based inference to transient noise in gravitational-wave ringdowns
summary: Gravitational waves (GW) emitted by binary systems allow us to perform precision tests of general relativity in the strong field regime. Ringdown signals allow for probing black hole mass and spin with high precision in GW astronomy. With improvements in current and next-generation GW detectors, developing likelihood-free parameter inference methods is crucial. This is especially important when facing challenges such as non-standard noise, partial data, or incomplete signal models that prevent the use of analytical likelihood functions. In this work, we propose an amortized simulation-based inference strategy to estimate ringdown parameters directly. Specifically, our method is based on amortized neural posterior estimation, which trains a neural density estimator of the posterior for all data segments within the prior range. The results show that our trained amortized network achieves statistically consistent parameter estimates with valid confidence coverage compared to established Markov-chain methods, while offering inference speeds that are orders of magnitude faster. Furthermore, we evaluate the robustness of the method against transient noise contamination. Our analysis reveals that the timing of glitch injection has a decisive impact on estimation bias, particularly during the tail of a signal with sparse information. Glitch strength is positively correlated with estimation error, but has limited effect at low signal-to-noise ratios. Mass and spin parameters are most sensitive to noise. This study not only provides an efficient and accurate inference framework for ringdown analysis but also lays a foundation for developing robust data-processing pipelines for future GW astronomy in realistic noise environments.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an amortized neural posterior estimation method for inferring gravitational wave ringdown parameters, offering faster and statistically valid results compared to traditional methods, while analyzing robustness against noise.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in GW parameter estimation with non-standard noise, partial data, or incomplete models where analytical likelihoods are infeasible.
AI.method: Uses amortized simulation-based inference with a neural density estimator trained on all data segments within the prior range for posterior estimation.
AI.result: The method provides statistically consistent parameter estimates with valid confidence coverage, faster inference speeds, and reveals that noise timing and strength affect estimation bias and error, particularly impacting mass and spin parameters.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables efficient ringdown analysis and establishes a foundation for robust GW data pipelines in realistic noise scenarios.

title: Gravitational baryogenesis beyond the spectator approximation
summary:
The standard gravitational-baryogenesis operator $λ\,
abla_μR\,J^μ$, with $λ\equiv ε/M_\ast^{2}$, is usually treated as a spectator interaction that generates an effective chemical potential in a prescribed background. When included in the gravitational action, however, it defines a genuine curvature--matter-coupling variational problem, relevant for the baryon, lepton, and $B\!-\!L$ currents, whether described microscopically by particle-physics operators or macroscopically by a fluid current $J^μ=n_Xu^μ$. Up to a boundary term the interaction is equivalent to $-λR
abla_μJ^μ$, making its $f(R,{m matter})$ character manifest, but the metric equations remain open unless the metric dependence of $J^μ$ is specified. For an arbitrary local realization $J^μ(Ψ,g)$ we derive the universal part of the field equations and isolate the realization-dependent tensor generated by $δ_g J^μ$. In the vector-density realization the explicit $J^α
abla_αR$ term cancels, but an algebraic term $-λg_{μν}R
abla_αJ^α$ survives, so the theory admits only a partial effective-Planck-mass interpretation, $M_{m eff}^2=M_{m Pl}^2-2λ
abla_μJ^μ$, and a time-dependent effective gravitational coupling during baryogenesis. Specializing to flat FRW with a homogeneous current $J^μ=n_Xu^μ$, we obtain the modified Friedmann and Raychaudhuri equations, the associated continuity relation, and dimensionless diagnostics that quantify when the spectator approximation is controlled. We also discuss the implications for gravitational-baryogenesis studies in modified theories of gravity, providing a consistent GR-side baseline for implementations in both standard cosmology and modified-gravity frameworks.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the gravitational-baryogenesis operator's role in modified gravity theories, showing it induces an effective Planck mass and time-varying gravitational coupling during baryogenesis. It derives modified Friedmann equations and provides diagnostics for when the spectator approximation holds.
AI.motivation: To understand the full variational implications of the gravitational-baryogenesis operator in curved spacetime, moving beyond the spectator approximation and ensuring consistency across different gravity frameworks.
AI.method: Analyzing the operator's contribution to the metric field equations for arbitrary local realizations of the current $J^μ$, deriving universal equations and realization-dependent terms. Specialized to flat Friedmann-Robertson-Walker (FRW) cosmology with homogeneous currents.
AI.result: Demonstrated that the operator modifies the effective gravitational coupling and Planck mass, derives modified cosmological equations, and provides diagnostics for approximation validity. Shows the operator's significance in both standard and modified gravity contexts.
AI.conclusion: The gravitational-baryogenesis operator cannot be treated purely as a spectator effect; its full coupling must be considered in cosmological models to ensure accurate predictions of baryogenesis effects in the early universe and modified gravity scenarios.

title: Unified Flavor: Lattice Quantization, Chain Locality, and a Dynamical Origin of Hierarchical Yukawas
summary: We present Unified Flavor (UF), a framework that synthesizes the $B$-lattice flavor hierarchy with a dynamical realization based on TeV-scale vectorlike fermion (VLF) chains. Hierarchical Yukawa couplings arise from discrete ninths-quantized lattice exponents enforced by a single flavon $Φ$ with $ε\equiv\langleΦ\rangle/Λ=1/B$, $B=75/14$. Effective Yukawa entries are generated as algebraic path sums along nearest-neighbor chains of vectorlike quarks (VLQs), factorizing into entry, chain-propagation, and exit amplitudes controlled by the discrete gauge charges. A multi-messenger structure -- in which each Yukawa entry receives coherent contributions from several chain configurations -- generates O(1) complex coefficients whose phases are the physical origin of CP violation. We derive a general chain-inversion theorem, perform systematic perturbative diagonalization of both up- and down-type Yukawa textures, and show that the Cabibbo--Kobayashi--Maskawa (CKM) mixing hierarchy and CP-phase structure emerge naturally from the lattice exponent algebra and multi-messenger interference. All six quark masses are reproduced with O(1) coefficients that are essentially unity. The chain locality simultaneously suppresses dangerous flavor-changing neutral currents (FCNCs) and satisfies electroweak precision constraints, while requiring VLQs with masses in the multi-TeV range accessible at the High-Luminosity Large Hadron Collider (HL-LHC). The same discrete gauge symmetry that enforces the lattice structure also protects the Peccei--Quinn axion quality, unifying flavor, CP violation, and the strong CP problem. The framework extends to the lepton sector, reproducing charged-lepton mass hierarchies, the normal-ordered neutrino spectrum, and PMNS mixing with a predictive two-branch octant--$δ$ correlation testable at DUNE and Hyper-Kamiokande.

AI.tldr: Unified Flavor (UF) is a framework that combines the $B$-lattice flavor hierarchy with TeV-scale vectorlike fermion chains, explaining quark and lepton mass hierarchies, CKM/PMNS mixing, and CP violation through discrete gauge symmetries. It suppresses FCNCs, aligns with electroweak constraints, and predicts testable lepton mixing correlations at future experiments.
AI.motivation: To synthesize the $B$-lattice flavor hierarchy with dynamical TeV-scale vectorlike fermion interactions, addressing quark/lepton mass hierarchies, CKM/PMNS mixing angles, CP violation origins, and FCNC suppression while connecting to axion physics.
AI.method: Implementing hierarchical Yukawa couplings via discrete ninths-quantized lattices driven by a single flavon $Φ$, using path sums over VLQ chain configurations to generate effective Yukawa matrices. Applies chain-inversion theorems for diagonalization and analyzes multi-messenger interference effects.
AI.result: Reproduces all quark masses with O(1) coefficients, naturally obtains CKM hierarchy and CP phases, predicts lepton mixing with a testable octant-$δ$ correlation, and ensures FCNC suppression/HL-LHC VLQ reach.
AI.conclusion: UF unifies flavor structure, CP violation, and axion solutions under a single discrete gauge symmetry, providing a testable framework for flavor physics with connections to multi-TeV physics and future neutrino experiments.

title: SN 2023axu: A Type IIP Supernova Interacted with a Low-Density Stellar Wind1
comment: Accepted for publication in APJ, 24 pages, 16 figures
summary: We present photometric and spectroscopic observations of Type IIP supernova SN 2023axu, spanning $\sim$400 d after the explosion. Its light curve is typical of normal SNe IIP, with a V-band peak of $-17.25 \pm 0.06$ mag and no early-time excess indicative of strong circumstellar interaction. The early spectra exhibit a distinctive broad "ledge" near 4600 Å. Through spectral modeling and comparison, we attribute this feature to a blend of C, N, and He lines excited by weak interaction between the ejecta and a low-density stellar wind. The late-time photometric evolution shows no discernible contribution from interaction, arguing against strong late-time circumstellar material engagement and supporting the low-density wind scenario. From modeling, this SN synthesized $\sim 0.055\,M_\odot$ of $^{56}$Ni, and nebular spectrum analysis indicates a progenitor mass near $15\,M_\odot$. SN 2023axu thus exemplifies weak ejecta-wind interaction and highlights the diversity of mass-loss histories and circumstellar environments of SNe II progenitors.

AI.tldr: SN 2023axu is a normal Type IIP supernova with weak ejecta-wind interaction, showing a 15 solar mass progenitor and minimal late-time circumstellar interaction.
AI.motivation: To study the properties and circumstellar environment of SN IIP progenitors through detailed photometric and spectroscopic analysis.
AI.method: Analyzed ~400 days of photometric and spectral data, performed spectral modeling to identify emission features (e.g., the 4600 Å ledge), and used nebular spectrum analysis to estimate progenitor mass and nickel yield.
AI.result: Observed weak early interaction via a spectral feature attributed to C/N/He lines, no late-time interaction, 0.055 solar masses of 56Ni synthesized, and a 15 solar mass progenitor.
AI.conclusion: SN 2023axu demonstrates diverse mass-loss histories in SN II progenitors, with its weak interaction supporting a low-density stellar wind scenario rather than strong circumstellar material engagement.

title: Screened Simpson-Visser Black Holes with Asymptotically de-Sitter Core
comment: 20 pages, 21 figures, 3 tables. Comments are welcome
summary: In this work, we introduce a screened Simpson-Visser regular solution and perform a comprehensive study of its physical and observational properties. We begin by analyzing the thermodynamic behavior of the black hole, including detailed investigations of the Hawking temperature, Gibbs free energy, and specific heat, which provide insights into its stability and phase structure. Next, we examine the geodesic structure of the spacetime, considering both massless (photon) and massive (timelike) particles. In particular, we study the photon sphere, the corresponding black hole shadow, and the innermost stable circular orbits (ISCO), which are crucial for understanding the motion of matter and light around the black hole. Furthermore, we explore the black hole's energy-emission rate radiation, highlighting the effects of the modified geometry on observational signatures. Finally, we investigate the topological aspects of the black hole, analyzing both the thermodynamic topology and the photon sphere's topological properties. Our analysis demonstrates the intricate interplay between the spacetime geometry, geodesic motion, and black hole thermodynamics, offering a deeper understanding of this class of regular black holes and their potential observational consequences.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of a screened Simpson-Visser regular black hole solution, exploring its thermodynamic properties, geodesic structure, energy emission, and topological aspects to understand its stability, phase behavior, and observational signatures.
AI.motivation: To investigate the physical and observational properties of a novel regular black hole solution to uncover insights into its stability, motion of particles, and potential observability.
AI.method: The authors analyze thermodynamic quantities (Hawking temperature, Gibbs free energy, specific heat), study photon spheres, black hole shadows, ISCOs, energy emission rates, and topological features of the spacetime.
AI.result: The analysis reveals the interplay between spacetime geometry, particle motion, and thermodynamics, showing how modifications to the geometry affect observational properties like shadows and emission spectra.
AI.conclusion: The screened Simpson-Visser black hole provides a rich framework for studying regular black holes, with implications for observational astronomy and the understanding of gravitational physics in modified geometries.

title: CP violation in two meson tau decays
comment: 13 pages, 5 figures, submitted to the proceedings of the 7th International Workshop on Future Tau Charm Facilities (23-27 Nov. 2025, Shanghai, China) to be published in International Journal of Modern Physics A (IJMPA)
summary: CP violation in $τ\to K_S πν_τ$ decays has attracted a lot of attention recently, due to the BaBar anomaly in the corresponding rate asymmetry. Within an effective field theory formalism, only extreme fine-tuning would allow to understand such measurement, which is currently being scrutinized at Belle(-II), as will be in the future super-charm-tau factory. Here we summarize the results of applying the same formalism to the other two-meson tau decay channels, which can help solve this conundrum. Our main conclusion is that current and future experiments would be sensitive to the maximum allowed CP rate asymmetry in the related $K^\pm K_S$ modes with a measurement having 5$\%$ precision, that will either support or cast further doubts on the BaBar anomaly.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates CP violation in τ→Kπν decays, focusing on resolving the BaBar anomaly by extending analysis to other two-meson channels. It concludes that future experiments with 5% precision can test the anomaly's validity.
AI.motivation: To address the BaBar anomaly observed in the rate asymmetry of τ→K_S πν_τ decays and explore whether the anomaly is supported or disproven through analysis of related decay modes.
AI.method: Uses effective field theory formalism applied to other two-meson tau decay channels (like K±K_S) to assess the feasibility of detecting CP violation signals with current/future experimental precision.
AI.result: Current and future experiments can measure maximum allowed CP asymmetry in K±K_S modes with 5% precision, which could either validate or challenge the BaBar anomaly.
AI.conclusion: The analysis suggests that high-precision measurements will clarify the origin of the BaBar anomaly, either confirming new physics or indicating systematic errors in previous results.

title: Revisiting early afterglows of gamma-ray bursts with finite-thickness ejecta: Implications from XRF 080330 and GRB 080710
summary: We revisit the physical origin of the achromatic peaks and breaks observed several thousand seconds after the burst in the multi-wavelength afterglows of XRF 080330 and GRB 080710. Using a numerical afterglow model that consistently incorporates finite ejecta thickness and a generalized external density profile, we perform Bayesian inference to estimate model parameters describing these events. Our analysis shows that the gradual rise and achromatic temporal features in both events are more naturally explained by jet dynamical evolution with finite shell thickness rather than by off-axis viewing effects. The inferred initial radial width of the ejecta is of order $10^{13}$ cm for both bursts, implying a central engine activity timescale significantly longer than that suggested by the prompt gamma-ray duration alone. Taken together, these results demonstrate that early afterglow light curves are strongly influenced by transition dynamics when finite ejecta thickness is properly taken into account, thereby providing a physical link between the prompt and afterglow phases and highlighting limitations of simply applying the thin-shell approximation when interpreting early-time afterglows. Furthermore, Bayesian model comparison favors a generalized circumburst density profile over the canonical uniform or steady-wind models, suggesting that fixing the external density structure to idealized profiles a priori may obscure crucial information about the progenitor's pre-burst activity.

AI.tldr: This study challenges the thin-shell approximation in explaining afterglow features of XRF 080330 and GRB 080710, showing that ejecta thickness and evolving jet dynamics are critical. Bayesian analysis supports finite ejecta width (~10^13 cm) and a generalized external density profile, linking prompt and afterglow phases.
AI.motivation: To understand the physical origins of achromatic peaks/breaks in afterglows observed thousands of seconds post-burst, and address limitations of previous models (thin-shell assumption, off-axis viewing effects).
AI.method: Applied a numerical afterglow model with finite ejecta thickness and a generalized external density profile, using Bayesian inference for parameter estimation and model comparison.
AI.result: Finite ejecta thickness better explains gradual rises/achromatic features than off-axis effects; inferred ejecta widths (~1e13 cm) indicate longer central engine activity than prompt gamma-ray durations. Generalized density profiles outperform uniform/steady-wind models.
AI.conclusion: Early afterglows are strongly influenced by ejecta dynamics with finite thickness, requiring abandonment of thin-shell approximation. Progenitor pre-burst activity is better inferred through non-idealized density profiles; this links prompt/afterglow phases physically.

title: Freeze-in dark matter in neutron stars
summary: Every neutron star is born in the process of core-collapse supernova explosion that, for a brief moment, reproduces conditions of the early Universe with temperatures $T\sim O(30\rm\,MeV)$. We calculate the production of Dark Matter $χ$ from the SM particles in such events, SM $\toχ\barχ$, for the freeze-in range of couplings, $α_{\rm FI} \sim O(10^{-26}) $, finding that $O(10^{-6})$ $χ$'s per nucleon is produced. The strong gravitational potential well of the neutron star retains a substantial fraction of these particles that will eventually undergo the reverse process of energy injection, $χ\barχ\to$ SM. This may lead to the abnormal energy injection creating observable signatures such as late-time heating of the neutron stars. To demonstrate the power of this method, we construct a set of simple dark matter models coupled to lepton currents, and show that neutron stars provide unique constraints on parameter space that otherwise cannot be accessed by other means, probing effectively the scattering cross sections with the SM in the ballpark of $σ_{χ\,\rm SM} \propto O(10^{-70})\,\rm cm^2$.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how neutron stars produce dark matter particles (χ) during supernova explosions and retain them, leading to potential late-time energy injection and observational signatures. It highlights neutron stars as novel probes for dark matter with cross-sections around 10^-70 cm², inaccessible via other methods.
AI.motivation: To explore the production and subsequent energy release of dark matter particles in neutron stars, providing constraints on dark matter models with extremely weak interactions not detectable through conventional experiments.
AI.method: Calculate dark matter production (SM → χχ̄) during supernovae using freeze-in couplings (~1e-26), model retention in neutron stars' gravitational wells, and analyze energy injection via χχ̄ → SM processes. Construct simple lepton-coupled dark matter models.
AI.result: Dark matter production of ~1e-6 per nucleon; neutron stars retain significant particles leading to possible observable effects like heating. Constraints probe cross-sections ~1e-70 cm² beyond current experimental reach.
AI.conclusion: Neutron stars offer unique astrophysical probes for weakly interacting dark matter, revealing parameter spaces inaccessible to direct/indirect detection experiments.

title: Unveiling the biconical geometry of the outflow in the ultraluminous X-ray source NGC 5204 X-1
comment: 14 pages, 14 figures, accepted for publication in A&A
summary: Ultraluminous X-ray sources (ULXs) are non-nuclear X-ray binary systems that exceed the Eddington luminosity for a 10 Msun black hole. The majority of these sources are thought to be stellar-mass compact objects accreting at super-Eddington rates, exhibiting powerful relativistic winds. These winds have been identified through the detection of absorption lines with a blueshift as high as 0.3c and emission lines typically found at their laboratory wavelengths. In this work, we analysed the XMM-Newton data of the ULX NGC 5204 X-1, which has been observed to exhibit emission lines with a blueshift of about 0.3c. The aim of this study is to examine the geometry and physical properties of the accretion disc and the relativistic outflows. In addition, we aim to explore the factors that influence the ULX spectral transitions. We undertook an observing campaign with XMM-Newton to explore the source behaviour at different luminosities. In this first paper of the series, we performed high-resolution X-ray spectroscopy, including archival data, with the RGS instrument which allowed us to resolve both emission and absorption lines. The outflows features were characterised using physical models of plasma in collisional-ionisation and photoionisation equilibrium. We identify collisionally-ionised blueshifted and redshifted components at about 0.3c. These findings have high statistical significance and suggest a biconical structure for the outflow. Additionally, the analysis of the O VII line triplet observed in the spectrum enables us to infer physical properties of the low-velocity line-emitting plasma, e.g. electron density (ne $\sim 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$) and temperature (Te $ \geq 1.5 \times 10^5$ K). A hybrid plasma whose ionisation balance is affected by both collisions and radiation is favoured.

AI.tldr: The paper examines an ultraluminous X-ray source (ULX NGC 5204 X-1) to study super-Eddington accretion outflows using XMM-Newton data. It identifies collisionally ionised biconical outflows and determines plasma properties like electron density (1e10 cm^-3) and temperature (~1.5e5 K).
AI.motivation: To understand the geometry, physical properties of accretion discs, and relativistic outflows in ULXs, and explore factors driving spectral transitions, particularly focusing on blueshifted emission/absorption features observed at 0.3c.
AI.method: Analysed XMM-Newton/RGS archival data with high-resolution spectroscopy. Applied models for collisional ionisation and photoionisation to characterize outflow features. Examined O VII line triplet for plasma property estimation.
AI.result: Identified highly significant biconical outflow structure with blueshifted/redshifted components at 0.3c. Determined electron density ~1e10 cm^-3 and temperature ≥1.5e5 K for low-velocity plasma, indicating a hybrid ionisation state from both collisions and radiation.
AI.conclusion: The biconical outflow geometry and hybrid ionisation suggest ULXs can sustain super-Eddington accretion via such structured winds. Observed plasma properties provide insights into accretion physics and ULX spectral variability mechanisms.

title: Topological field theory plus local Lorentz symmetry is gravity
summary: Four-dimensional gravity admits many equivalent formulations - metric, Einstein-Cartan, teleparallel, McDowell-Mansouri, among others - each offering distinct advantages, particularly, in view of quantization. We propose a new formulation based on Weyl spinor-valued 1-forms, ultimately encoding the frame-field data. Starting from a topological field theory with a global $\mathrm{SL}(2,\mathbb{C})$ symmetry, we show that promoting this symmetry to a local gauge symmetry leads to the emergence of gravity. We analyze the covariant phase space of this theory, its symmetries and charge structure and explore the role of admissible corner terms together with their impact on boundary charges and their algebra. We study several extensions of this framework, including the incorporation of a cosmological constant and a novel $ G \rightarrow 0 $ scaling limit obtained from this model. The presence of the frame field already at the topological level allows point particles to be coupled uniformly in both the topological and gravitational theories. We perform a detailed Hamiltonian analysis of the theory and clarify the implementation of the reality conditions. We argue that this formulation provides structural features that make it particularly well suited for both discretization and quantization.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new Weyl spinor-valued 1-form formulation of four-dimensional gravity starting from a topological field theory, exploring gauge symmetry promotion to gravity, phase space analysis, cosmological extensions, and quantization prospects.
AI.motivation: To find an equivalent formulation of gravity that facilitates quantization by leveraging Weyl spinors and topological field theories, offering advantages over existing frameworks like metric or teleparallel approaches.
AI.method: Begin with a topological field theory with global SL(2,C) symmetry, promoting it to local gauge symmetry to derive gravity equations. Analyze covariant phase space, symmetries, charge structure, corner terms, boundary charges, and algebra. Extend to include cosmological constant and G→0 scaling. Include Hamiltonian analysis and reality conditions.
AI.result: Demonstrated emergence of gravity from topological theory via gauge promotion. Showed uniform coupling of point particles in topological and gravitational phases. Established framework's suitability for discretization/quantization through detailed phase space and Hamiltonian analysis.
AI.conclusion: The new formulation provides structural advantages for quantization efforts due to its foundation in topological field theory, gauge symmetry handling, and coherent phase space properties, positioning it as a promising approach for quantum gravity research.

title: Transverse Structure of the Kaon: A light-front Hamiltonian Approach
summary: We employ the Basis Light-Front Quantization (BLFQ) framework to compute the leading-twist (twist-2) and subleading-twist (twist-3) transverse-momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDs) of the kaon. The light-front wave functions are obtained by diagonalizing a light-front QCD Hamiltonian that includes quark-antiquark (|q\bar{q}\rangle) and quark-antiquark-gluon (|q\bar{q}g\rangle) Fock components together with a three-dimensional confinement. Using the QCD equations of motion, the twist-3 TMDs are decomposed into twist-2 constributions and genuine twist-3 terms, the latter encoding quark-quark-gluon correlations beyond the probabilistic picture. These genuine twist-3 constributions arise from the interference between the |q\bar{q}\rangle and |q\bar{q}g\rangle sectors, which are usually negelected in the Wandzura-Wilczek approximation. This work provides the first theoretical predictions of kaon subleading-twist TMDs that explicitly account for Fock-sector interference. In addition, we present results for the kaon's twist-2 and twist-3 collinear parton distribution functions (PDFs). The twist-2 PDFs are found to be in good agreement with the recent global analysis by the JAM collaboration.

AI.tldr: The paper uses BLFQ to calculate twist-2 and twist-3 TMDs and collinear PDFs for the kaon. It introduces Fock-sector interference terms beyond the Wandzura-Wilczek approximation, providing first predictions for kaon twist-3 TMDs and validates twist-2 PDFs against JAM analysis.
AI.motivation: To compute leading/subleading twist TMDs and collinear PDFs for kaons, and explore quark-quark-gluon correlations beyond conventional approximations.
AI.method: Uses BLFQ framework with LFWFs diagonalizing a light-front QCD Hamiltonian including |q ar{q}angle and |q ar{q}gangle components plus 3D confinement. Decomposes twist-3 TMDs into twist-2 and genuine twist-3 terms using QCD equations of motion.
AI.result: First theoretical predictions for kaon twist-3 TMDs considering Fock-sector interference. Twist-2 PDFs match JAM collaboration results. Genuine twist-3 contributions arise from |q ar{q}angle/|q ar{q}gangle interference.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates necessity of including Fock-state mixing effects for accurate TMD descriptions. Validates method through PDF comparison, advancing predictive capabilities for subleading-twist dynamics.

title: The twin-jet system in the FRII radio galaxy 3C 452: A sub-parsec scale VLBI study
comment: Main: 11 pages, 9 figures. Appendix: 5 pages, 3 figures. Accepted for publication in A&A
summary: We present a comprehensive multifrequency VLBI analysis of the FRII, high-excitation radio galaxy 3C 452, aiming to resolve and analyze for the first time its twin-jet structure on sub-parsec scales. Our data set comprises High Sensitivity Array (HSA) observations at 4.9, 8.4, 15.4, 23.6, and 43.2 GHz. Through fitting methods performed in both the visibility and the image plane, we trace the jet expansion from scales of a few thousand to nearly $10^5$ Schwarzschild radii ($R_S$) on both the approaching and receding jets. Additionally, we derive the core brightness temperatures and Doppler factors to constrain the jet's orientation and intrinsic speed. Our study provides the first detailed description of the twin-jet system in 3C 452 on VLBI scales, confirming it as a rare FRII source with jets detected down to millimeter wavelengths. We resolve both jet and counter-jet down to scales of a few thousand $R_S$, revealing a symmetric, parabolically expanding structure with power-law indices $k \approx 0.66$ (jet) and $k \approx 0.47$ (counter-jet). The brightness temperature analysis yields low Doppler factors ($δ\sim 0.03$-$0.83$), indicative of Doppler de-boosting due to the large viewing angle ($θ\approx 70^\circ$) and/or a magnetically dominated jet base. A spectral index analysis reveals a strongly inverted core spectrum ($α> 2$) with additional absorption at the highest frequencies, followed by a sharp steepening ($α\sim -2.5$) to optically thin values in the innermost jet. Finally, a comparison between broad- and narrow-line high-excitation radio galaxies shows that jets in narrow-line sources such as 3C 452 and Cygnus A complete collimation at $\leq 10^5 R_S$, whereas broad-line sources exhibit shape transitions at $10^6$-$10^7 R_S$, suggesting that orientation plays an important role in the observed collimation scales.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first detailed analysis of the twin-jet structure of the FRII radio galaxy 3C 452 on sub-parsec scales using multifrequency VLBI observations. The study reveals symmetric, parabolically expanding jets with low Doppler factors, indicating a large viewing angle and magnetic dominance. Comparisons with other galaxies suggest orientation influences jet collimation scales.
AI.motivation: The motivation was to resolve and analyze the twin-jet structure of 3C 452 on sub-parsec scales for the first time, particularly focusing on understanding jet dynamics, collimation, and the role of orientation in FRII radio galaxies.
AI.method: The authors used High Sensitivity Array (HSA) VLBI observations at multiple frequencies (4.9 to 43.2 GHz). They applied fitting methods in visibility and image planes to trace jet expansion across scales from thousands to ~1e5 Schwarzschild radii. They also analyzed brightness temperatures, Doppler factors, and spectral indices to infer jet orientation and composition.
AI.result: Key results include the first detection of twin jets in 3C 452 down to millimeter wavelengths, symmetric parabolic expansion with power-law indices ~0.66 and ~0.47 for the jet/counter-jet. Low Doppler factors (δ~0.03–0.83) suggest large viewing angles (~70°) and magnetically dominated jets. Spectral analysis showed an inverted core spectrum with absorption at high frequencies. comparisons indicated narrow-line FRII sources collimate jets at ~1e5 R_S, differing from broad-line sources.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes 3C 452 as a rare FRII source with millimeter-scale jet detection, highlighting orientation's role in collimation scales. The findings support models where magnetic fields influence jet structure and viewing angle determines observable jet properties.

title: Shifted-geodesic approximation for spinning-body gravitational wave fluxes
comment: 55 pages, 12 figures. Submitted to Classical and Quantum Gravity
summary: We present a shifted-geodesic framework for computing gravitational-wave fluxes from spinning test bodies moving on bound orbits of Kerr black holes. The method provides a simple and efficient means of evaluating energy and angular momentum fluxes incorporating the leading effect of the smaller body's spin. Because post-adiabatic corrections, including secondary spin contributions, are subdominant to the leading adiabatic terms, this approximation is well justified. In particular, we find that oscillatory spin terms typically contribute very little to fluxes, but their contribution to the description of orbits is computationally expensive, making such terms a natural target for approximation. In our framework, orbital frequencies and integrals of the motion are perturbed to include spin effects, while the trajectory retains the global structure of geodesic motion. This simplifies the computation of gravitational radiation. The shifted-geodesic approximation is most reliable for orbits with lower eccentricity, lower inclination, and larger semi-latus recta. The approximation becomes less reliable as we approach the separatrix between stable and unstable orbits; fortunately, many inspirals spend less time in this region of parameter space. A diagnostic inspiral evolution shows very small dephasing due to use of the shifted-geodesic approximation ($\approx10^{-2}$ radians over 1 year), confirming that spin-induced flux corrections can be accurately included using this simple modification to a geodesic trajectory. This approximation provides a rapid and convenient way to compute spinning-body orbits, but is not intended to replace more accurate treatments. We propose it as a pragmatic alternative when speed and simplicity are prioritized, enabling efficient EMRI/IMRI flux calculations and supporting parameter-space studies for LISA. (Abridged)

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a shifted-geodesic framework to efficiently compute gravitational-wave fluxes from spinning test bodies around Kerr black holes, focusing on orbits with lower eccentricity, inclination, and larger semi-latus recta. The method simplifies calculations by retaining geodesic trajectory structures while incorporating spin effects into orbital parameters, showing minimal dephasing over a year.
AI.motivation: To develop a computationally efficient method for calculating gravitational-wave fluxes for spinning bodies in Kerr spacetime, avoiding the computational expense of including secondary spin terms while maintaining accuracy for most inspiral scenarios relevant to LISA observations.
AI.method: The shifted-geodesic approximation perturbs orbital frequencies and integrals of motion to include leading-order spin effects, while keeping the trajectory's global structure geodesic. This avoids expensive computations of oscillatory spin contributions to orbits but retains their effects on fluxes.
AI.result: The method achieves minimal dephasing (~1e-2 radians/year) and reliably approximates fluxes for orbits away from the separatrix. It is less accurate near unstable orbits but suitable for rapid EMRI/IMRI flux computations and parameter-space studies.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a pragmatic balance between speed and accuracy for spin-incorporating flux calculations, ideal for large-scale studies with LISA, though not replacing higher-fidelity methods for detailed analyses.

title: Energy-momentum tensor form factors and spin density distribution in the nucleon calculated in a quantized Skyrme model with vector mesons
summary: We investigate energy-momentum tensor (EMT) form factors and the spatial spin density distribution in the nucleon within a framework of the quantized Skyrme model with vector mesons. We construct both the canonical and Belinfante improved EMTs and analyze how pseudogauge uncertainty influences local spin and momentum densities while leaving the global nucleon properties unchanged. Using the inversion formulas from nucleon matrix elements in the forward limit, we extract the form factors, $A(t)$, $D(t)$, and $J(t)$, in both pseudogauges and the additional antisymmetric form factor associated with the canonical EMT. We find that the pseudogauge choice leads to sizable differences in the local spin and momentum densities. In particular, the canonical EMT naturally encodes spin density through the antisymmetric tensor structure, while the Belinfante EMT is sensitive to the total angular momentum only. Our results illustrate explicitly how different pseudogauges correspond to different spatial interpretations of nucleon spin structure within the same underlying dynamics. These findings provide a concrete model realization of the pseudogauge ambiguity in QCD-inspired nucleon structure and offer useful intuition for interpreting spatial distributions.

AI.tldr: The paper explores energy-momentum tensor form factors and spatial spin density in the nucleon using a Skyrme model with vector mesons, showing how pseudogauge choices affect local spin and momentum distributions.
AI.motivation: To clarify the pseudogauge ambiguity in nucleon structure by analyzing how different pseudogauges influence local spin and momentum densities without altering global nucleon properties.
AI.method: Constructs canonical and Belinfante improved EMTs in a Skyrme model framework, extracts form factors (A(t), D(t), J(t)) via inversion formulas, and compares pseudogauge-dependent spatial distributions.
AI.result: Pseudogauge choices lead to significant differences in local spin/momentum densities; canonical EMT encodes spin via antisymmetric tensors while Belinfante EMT reflects total angular momentum, demonstrating explicit spatial interpretations aligned with QCD-inspired dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The model resolves pseudogauge ambiguity's impact on nucleon spin structure, offering insights for interpreting spatial spin distributions in QCD contexts.

title: Sensitivity to Axion-like Particle dark matter with very-high-energy gamma-ray observations of Active Galactic Nuclei located behind Galaxy Clusters
summary:
Axion-Like-Particles (ALPs) are hypothetical pseudo-scalar particles actively searched as light dark matter candidates. The coupling of ALPs to photons can give rise to distinctive spectral features in the observed gamma-ray spectrum of astrophysical sources.
  We perform a forecast study on the sensitivity to ALP-photon interactions using stacked mock observations of selected active galactic nuclei (AGNs) located behind galaxy clusters (GC). The ALP-photon conversion in the magnetic fields of galaxy clusters give rise to absorption-like features in AGN spectra that are subject to large variance in their prediction for individual sources. We consider here a stacking analysis of multiple AGN-cluster pairs, which yields a more controlled prediction of the expected ALP-induced spectral patterns in the observed gamma-ray spectra.
  Using realistic mock observations of selected Fermi-LAT AGNs by ongoing Imaging Atmospheric Cherenkov Telescopes such as H.E.S.S., MAGIC and VERITAS, we provide a careful assessment of the expected sensitivity of a combined statistical analysis of many AGN-GC pairs, together with the impact of modelling and instrumental uncertainties. The sensitivity reaches ALP-photon couplings down to 6$\times$10$^{-13}$ GeV$^{-1}$ for an ALP mass of 3$\times$10$^{-8}$ eV, and is currently statistically dominated indicating further improvements from more observations. Such a stacking analysis approach enables exploration of the yet-uncharted ALP dark matter parameter space in the 10$^{-8}$ - 10$^{-7}$ eV mass range.

AI.tldr: The study explores the sensitivity to Axion-Like-Particle (ALP)-photon interactions using stacked mock observations of AGN-GC pairs, achieving couplings down to 6×10⁻¹³ GeV⁻¹ for ALP masses around 3×10⁻⁸ eV, and highlights stacking's potential to explore uncharted ALP dark matter parameter spaces.
AI.motivation: ALPs are promising dark matter candidates with detectable photon coupling effects. However, individual AGN-GC pair analyses have high prediction variance. Stacking multiple observations could reduce uncertainty and improve sensitivity in detecting ALP-induced spectral features.
AI.method: A stacking analysis of mock gamma-ray observations from selected AGNs behind galaxy clusters using current IACTs (H.E.S.S., MAGIC, VERITAS). Models ALP-photon conversion in GC magnetic fields, accounts for instrumental and astrophysical uncertainties, and evaluates statistical sensitivities.
AI.result: The stacking method achieves sensitivity to ALP couplings as low as 6×10⁻¹³ GeV⁻¹ at 3×10⁻⁸ eV mass. Sensitivity is statistically limited, suggesting improvements with more data. This approach opens exploration of ALP masses between 10⁻⁸–10⁻⁷ eV, previously inaccessible.
AI.conclusion: Stacking analyses significantly enhance detection prospects for ALPs. Future observations with existing or next-gen telescopes could map unexplored ALP parameter spaces, offering critical tests for dark matter models.

title: Overdamping of Neutron-Mirror-Neutron Transitions in Neutron Stars
summary: The neutron to mirror neutron transitions in neutron stars would possibly result in significant effects. In this work we show that collisional decoherence entails exponential relaxation in lieu of oscillations. Decoherence is a great many orders of magnitude faster than the expected oscillations. The admixture of mirror neutrons at all times remains very small with respect to ordinary neutrons component.

AI.tldr: The study investigates neutron to mirror neutron transitions in neutron stars and finds that collisional decoherence causes exponential relaxation instead of oscillations, with decoherence occurring far faster than expected oscillation timescales, resulting in a negligible mirror neutron component.
AI.motivation: To explore the possible effects of neutron to mirror neutron transitions in neutron stars and understand the role of decoherence in these processes.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of collisional decoherence effects on neutron oscillations, comparing the timescales of decoherence and oscillations.
AI.result: Decoherence occurs many orders of magnitude faster than oscillations, leading to exponential relaxation rather than oscillatory behavior, and mirror neutrons remain a very small admixture.
AI.conclusion: Mirror neutron transitions in neutron stars are strongly suppressed due to rapid collisional decoherence, limiting their observable effects.

title: X-ray evidence from NuSTAR for a Mach 3 shock in Merging Galaxy Cluster ZWCL 1856.8
comment: 15 Pages, 9 Figures, 2 Tables. Revision submitted to ApJ
summary: We present spectral analysis results of deeper (270 ks) NuSTAR observations of the merging galaxy cluster system, ZWCL1856.8+6616, at redshift z=0.304, following a pilot study using shallower (30 ks) NuSTAR data (Tumer et al. 2024).
